---
title: "rep-sample-analysis"
format: html
bibliography: mocareferences.bib
citation-location: document
reference-location: section
editor: visual
---

```{r}
#| label: Data Fetching. Load R packages
#| include: false 

library(osfr) 
library(dplyr)
library(ez) 
library(tidyverse) 
library(gt)
library(gtsummary)
library(kableExtra)
library(stringr)
library(ggridges)
library(lubridate)  # for date handling
library(psych) # for factor analysis 
library(pheatmap) # for factor analysis heat map 
library(corrplot) # for correlation matrix 
library(broom) # for correlation tests
library(purrr) # for correlation tests 
library(likert) # for likert plots 
library(factoextra)
library(ggfortify)
library(car) 
library(GPArotation) # for factor analysis 
# for mlr
# library(GGally)
# library(car)
# library(MASS)
# library(glmnet)

```

```{r}
#| label: Prolific demographics data
#| include: false 

## add which round of the pilot it was by reading in demographics files so we know completion dates

#read in prolific demographics files 
demo_df <- read_csv("prolific_demographic_export_6978ff4366f4253e5b853496.csv") %>%
  mutate(Age = as.numeric(Age))

num_no_code_subjects_df <- nrow(demo_df %>%
  filter(`Completion code` == "NOCODE") %>% 
  group_by(`Participant id`) %>%
  summarize(num_no_code_subjects = n()))

#clean df
cleaned_prolific_demographics_df <- demo_df %>%
# make name the same as in data csvs so merging is easier
rename("prolific_subject_id" = "Participant id") %>%
# filter out people who didn't finish
filter(Status != "RETURNED") %>%
#rename "Started at" to make grepl function easier to use later on
rename("start" = "Started at") %>%
# make time more readable but keep the seconds version for analysis stuff later
mutate(`time_taken_overall_minutes` = as.numeric(`Time taken`) / 60) %>%
#remove the no code and unknown code people 
  filter(`Completion code` == "C12ZD3N0")

str(cleaned_prolific_demographics_df)

```

```{r}
#| label: Retrieve osf data
#| include: false

# loading in the data after manually downloading from osf and putting in a folder called data 
# not using osf retrieval because osf retrieval wasn't grabbing all the csvs:
file_names <- list.files("data/", pattern = "\\.csv$", full.names = FALSE)

raw_dfs_tibbles <- map(file_names, ~read_csv(file.path("data/", .)))

rawwwwwww_df <- data.table::rbindlist(raw_dfs_tibbles)
 

#note that fnxwd is the osf node for representative sample

```

```{r}
#| label: Verifying that we have all data 
#| include: false 

# verify that we have the data for all the people by making sure that the data df has as many subs as the demo df, and printing the missing id's if not 

difference <- setdiff(
  cleaned_prolific_demographics_df$prolific_subject_id,
  rawwwwwww_df$prolific_subject_id
)

if (length(difference) > 0) {
  print("MISSING DATA FROM PARTICIPANTS:")
  print(difference)
}

```

```{r}
#| label: Merge prolific demos + create a condition type column
#| include: false 

raw_df <- rawwwwwww_df %>%
  # inner join so we drop anyone from the osf data that wasn't in the demographics file (like test runs) and so we drop anyone in the prolific file who didn't make it to OSF
  inner_join(cleaned_prolific_demographics_df, by = "prolific_subject_id") %>%
  # remove unnecessary syntax from topicChoiceAsString for readability later
  mutate(topicChoiceAsString = gsub('\\{"row":"', '', topicChoiceAsString)) %>%
  mutate(topicChoiceAsString = gsub('"}', '', topicChoiceAsString)) %>%
  # fill condition column and row and topic strings
  group_by(prolific_subject_id) %>%
  fill(topicChoiceAsString, .direction = "downup") %>%
  ungroup() 

# number of subjects in data 
total_num_subjects_in_data <- nrow(raw_df %>%
  group_by(prolific_subject_id) %>%
  summarize(total_num_subjects_in_data = n()))

```

```{r}
#| label: Clean the survey df
#| include: false

surveys_cleaning1_df <- raw_df %>%
  # Filter for only surveys
  filter(trial_type == "survey") %>%
  # Remove control interventions (these are coded as surveys)
  filter(phase != "control-intervention") %>%
  # Remove the matrix question titles because they mess with separating questions from answers
  # openmindedness and unwillingness reason
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub(',"openmindedness":null,', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"openmindedness":', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub(',"unwillingness-reason":null,', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"unwillingness-reason":', '', .))) %>%
  # republican/democrat (pre-intervention)
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub(',"rating-republicans":null,', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub(',"rating-democrats":null,', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"rating-republicans":null,', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"rating-democrats":null,', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"interacting-with-republicans":null,', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"interacting-with-democrats":null,', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"interacting-with-republicans":null', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"interacting-with-democrats":null', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"rating-republicans":', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"rating-democrats":', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"interacting-with-republicans":', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"interacting-with-democrats":', '', .))) %>%
  # republican/democrat (post-intervention)
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"rating-republicans-post":null,', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"rating-democrats-post":null,', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"interacting-with-republicans-post":null,', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"interacting-with-democrats-post":null,', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"rating-republicans-post":', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"rating-democrats-post":', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"interacting-with-republicans-post":', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"interacting-with-democrats-post":', '', .))) %>%
  # the name of the question in 2.1 run of the experiment
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('topicChoice":', '', .))) %>%
  # the name of the question in future runs of the experiment
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('topic":', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub(',null,', '', .))) %>%
  # there is a bug (ish) in the slider feature in jspsych: the slider sits at 50 on the user end, but the data is entered as null unless the user moves the slider around (even if only to put it back at 50). four participants encountered this problem, so i'm going to correct their data 
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"slider1-placeholder":null,', '"slider1-placeholder":50,', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"slider2-placeholder":null,', '"slider2-placeholder":50,', .)))


surveys_cleaning2_df <- surveys_cleaning1_df %>%
  # Remove weird characters but don't remove quotations because we need them for separating questions
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('}', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('\\{', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('\\[', '', .))) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('\\]', '', .))) %>%
  # Make each question its own row
  separate_rows(response, sep = ',"') %>%
  # Remove any leftover quotation marks
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('"', '', .))) %>%
  # Move answers to a new column
  separate(response, into = c("question", "answer"), sep = ":", extra = "merge", fill = "right") %>%
  # Remove all remaining questions that were answered with null (like the slider questions, because the slider 'placeholder' questions are where people actually answer the slider questions)
  filter(!is.na(answer) & answer != 'null' & answer != 'null,null') %>%
  filter(question != "unwillingness-reason-")

```

```{r}
#| label: Reverse code necessary items 
#| include: false 

# reverse code open-mindedness items 

surveys_cleaning3_df <- surveys_cleaning2_df %>%
    mutate(answer = ifelse(
     grepl("@R@", question),
     8 - as.numeric(answer),
     (answer)
   ))

```

```{r}
#| label: Remove unnecessary columns 
#| include: false 

surveys_df <- surveys_cleaning3_df %>%
  select(-c(stimulus, trial_type, trial_index, time_elapsed, internal_node_id, study_id, session_id, unwillingness_yes_rows, "Submission id", Status, "Custom study tncs accepted at", "Reviewed at", "Archived at", "Completion code", "Total approvals"))

```

```{r}
#| label: Create a df that flags people who failed the attention check or commitment check
#| include: false


#see who passes the check. I'm including people who may have left the slider at 1 because this was almost certainly just an honest mistake in not moving the slider far enough 
attention_check <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(question == "attention-check-slider-pre-placeholder") %>%
  group_by(prolific_subject_id) %>%
  mutate(n_hits = sum(answer %in% c("0.00", "1.00")),
    flag = case_when(
      n_hits == 0 ~ "fail (single)",
      n_hits  > 0 ~ "pass",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_ )) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(flag, n_hits, question, answer, prolific_subject_id) 

#get list of all subject id's so we can see which subjects didn't even answer the question (the sliders aren't recorded in our df if the subject doesn't move them. they naturally sit at 50.) 
all_ids <- surveys_df %>%
  distinct(prolific_subject_id)

#filter for the question of interest
attention_ids <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(question == "attention-check-slider-pre-placeholder") %>%
  distinct(prolific_subject_id) 

#see who's missing
missing_ids <- setdiff(all_ids$prolific_subject_id, attention_ids$prolific_subject_id)

#make a table for these missing people 
missing_rows <- tibble(
  flag = "fail (single)",
  question = NA_character_,
  answer = NA_character_,
  prolific_subject_id = missing_ids,
  time_taken_overall_minutes = NA_real_
)

# combine those who answered and those who didn't answer
attention_check <- bind_rows(attention_check, missing_rows)

# extract value for text 
total_failed_attention_checks <- attention_check %>%
  # excluding people for both fails
  filter(flag == 'fail (single)') %>%
  summarize(total_failed_attention_checks = n_distinct(prolific_subject_id)) %>%
  pull(total_failed_attention_checks)

#extract list of ids so we can remove the data
failed_attention_check_ids <- attention_check %>%
  filter(flag == "fail (single)") %>%
  distinct(prolific_subject_id) %>%
  pull(prolific_subject_id)


# check the commitment check
fail_commitment_check <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(question == "commitment-check") %>%
  filter(answer != "yes")
```

```{r}
#| label: Remove subs who failed attention check 
#| include: false

# remove bad subs
raw_df <- raw_df %>%
  filter(!(prolific_subject_id %in% failed_attention_check_ids))
    
surveys_df <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(!prolific_subject_id %in% failed_attention_check_ids) 

total_num_clean_subjects_in_data <- nrow(raw_df %>%
  group_by(prolific_subject_id) %>%
  summarize(total_num_clean_subjects_in_data = n()))

```

```{r}
#| label: Print surveys_df csv (and txt for Claude)
#| echo: false 

write.csv(surveys_df, "rep_sample_survey_data.csv", row.names = FALSE)

write.table(surveys_df, file = "pretest_survey_data.txt", sep = "\t", row.names = FALSE, quote = FALSE)

```

## Introduction

Existing literature indicates that a complex interplay of psychological factors—including fear of social isolation (@NoelleNeumann1974; @Chen2018; @Haug2025), identity protection (@Tajfel1979; @Hunter1996), communication anxiety (@Beatty1987; @Schulenberg2023), and emotional dysregulation (@Masters2019; @Aldao2010)—converge with sociocultural influences to deter open dialogue (@Haug2025; @Kahneman2011; @Lewandowsky2017). This avoidance is a significant barrier to constructive discourse. Understanding this phenomenon is critical for fostering democratic citizenship and cultivating inclusive learning environments.

We are developing an individual-level intervention to help increase Americans' willingness to engage in conversations on divisive topics. To properly measure the barriers to engagement and assess the effectiveness of our intervention, we first designed a 25-item questionnaire to capture the core concerns that contribute to an individual's reluctance to engage in conversations on controversial topics. This pretest study aims to assess the effectiveness of this 25-item questionnaire through addressing two primary preregistered research questions:

Comprehensiveness: Do the 25 items in our questionnaire capture the primary concerns that contribute to Americans' unwillingness to engage in productive, open-minded discussions about divisive topics?

Structure: Are there any distinct, underlying factors that organize these 25 concerns?

## Methods

### Materials

We used custom software written with the jsPsych framework (@jspsychcite) to create the study, which participants viewed and responded to via their personal laptops or desktops. The javascript code for the experiment and RStudio code for data analysis can both be found at (<https://github.com/jodeleeuw/URSI-LLM-Convo-Bot/tree/main/pilot_1).> Pre-registrations and all data for this study and previous pilots are available on the Open Science Framework at <https://osf.io/x32pv.>

### Participants and Procedure

```{r}
#| label: Find time taken stats
#| echo: false 

#finding overall mean, min, max of how long participants took 
time_stats <- surveys_df %>%
  mutate(time_taken_overall_minutes = as.numeric(time_taken_overall_minutes)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  summarise(
    Mean = round(mean(time_taken_overall_minutes, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    Min = round(min(time_taken_overall_minutes, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    Max = round(max(time_taken_overall_minutes, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    Median = round(median(time_taken_overall_minutes, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
  ) 

mean_survey_time <- time_stats$Mean
min_survey_time <- time_stats$Min
max_survey_time <- time_stats$Max
median_survey_time <- time_stats$Median
```

```{r}
#| label:  tbl-basic-demographics-table
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: Table of basic demographics.

#reloading package because functions from other packages were overriding??
library(gtsummary)

#we already read in demographics earlier 

#create a demographics table from prolific screening demo data which we loaded onto the raw df earlier
survey_demographics_df <- surveys_df %>%
filter(question == "commitment-check") %>% # picking random question so there's only one row per subject
select(prolific_subject_id, Age, Sex, "Ethnicity simplified", "Employment status", time_taken_overall_minutes) %>%
  mutate(Age = as.numeric(Age))

survey_demographics_df <- survey_demographics_df %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.factor), ~ droplevels(.)))

survey_demographics_df$time_taken_overall_minutes <- round(
  as.numeric(survey_demographics_df$time_taken_overall_minutes), 
  1
)

demographics_table <- tbl_summary(survey_demographics_df,
 by = NULL,
  label = list(
    `time_taken_overall_minutes` = "Time taken (minutes)",
    Age = "Age (years)",
    Sex = "Sex",
    `Ethnicity simplified` = "Ethnicity"
  ),
  statistic = NULL,
#list(
  #  all_continuous() ~ "{mean} ({sd})",
  #  all_categorical() ~ "{n} ({p}%)"
#),
  type = list(
    `time_taken_overall_minutes` = "continuous",
    `Age` = "continuous",
    `gender-identification` = "categorical",
   `Sex` = "categorical",
    `Ethnicity simplified` = "categorical"
  ),
  digits = list(
    all_continuous() ~ 2,
    all_categorical() ~ 0
  ),
 include = c(
'time_taken_overall_minutes',
'Age',
'Sex',
'Ethnicity simplified'
))
 # %>%  modify_header(label = "**whatever label i want**")

# Print the table
as_gt(demographics_table)

```

Through Prolific, 300 subjects participated in this survey. Participants were restricted to U.S. residents who fluently speak English. Data from `r num_no_code_subjects_df` were not collected because the subjects did not wait for the Prolific completion code at the end of the survey. Data from `r length(difference)` subject is mysteriously missing from the OSF data folder despite the subject properly completing the survey. We excluded data from `r total_failed_attention_checks` subjects for failing the attention check question, leaving us with a final sample of `r total_num_clean_subjects_in_data` subjects. Participants completed the questionnaire in an average of `r mean_survey_time` minutes (Range: `r min_survey_time` – `r max_survey_time`).

Participants were first asked to rate their comfort level with 10 controversial topics. The topic that they were least willing to discuss was selected as their focus topic for the remainder of the survey. The full questionnaire included self-reported willingness to converse (via two slider questions from 0-100), self-reported open-mindedness (a 5-item Likert scale from 1-7), the 25-item concern inventory (a Likert scale from 1-7, along with an initial binary "Yes/No" endorsement). The session concluded with a free-response question about participants' reasons for reluctance, which we will assess for any uncaptured reasons.

## Results

### Basic results

```{r}
#| label: Make short topic labels
#| echo: false

# shortened topic labels for use in figures
topic_labels <- c( 
  "mandating vaccines in the U.S." = 
    "Vaccine Mandates",
  "human euthanasia in the U.S." = 
    "Human euthanasia",
  "deportation policies for undocumented immigrants in the U.S." = 
    "Deportation policies",
  "transgender athletes in U.S. sports" = 
    "Transgender athletes in sports",
  "the role of the U.S. government in healthcare" = 
    "Government in healthcare",
  "U.S. public policy on climate change" = 
    "Climate change policies",
  "the use of A.I. in traditionally human-run spaces (e.g., medicine, art, driving)" = 
    "A.I. in human-run spaces",
  "the role of capitalism in the U.S. economy" = 
    "Capitalism in economy",
  "the role of the U.S. in the Israeli-Palestinian conflict" = 
    "U.S. in Israeli-Palestinian conflict",
  "police conduct and accountability in the U.S." =
    "Police conduct and accountability"
)

# really short labels. should only use if necessary 
really_short_topic_labels <- c( 
  "mandating vaccines in the U.S." = 
    "Vaccines",
  "human euthanasia in the U.S." = 
    "Euthanasia",
  "deportation policies for undocumented immigrants in the U.S." = 
    "Deportation",
  "transgender athletes in U.S. sports" = 
    "Trans athletes",
  "the role of the U.S. government in healthcare" = 
    "Healthcare",
  "U.S. public policy on climate change" = 
    "Climate change",
  "the use of A.I. in traditionally human-run spaces (e.g., medicine, art, driving)" = 
    "A.I.",
  "the role of capitalism in the U.S. economy" = 
    "Capitalism",
  "the role of the U.S. in the Israeli-Palestinian conflict" = 
    "Israel-Palestine",
  "police conduct and accountability in the U.S." =
    "Police"
)


# make into dfs and add to surveys_df for ease of use  
short_topic_labels_lookup <- tibble(
  short_topic_labels = unlist(topic_labels),
  topicChoiceAsString = rep(names(topic_labels), lengths(topic_labels))
)

really_short_topic_labels_lookup <- tibble(
  really_short_short_topic_labels = unlist(really_short_topic_labels),
  topicChoiceAsString = rep(names(really_short_topic_labels), lengths(really_short_topic_labels))
)

surveys_df <- surveys_df %>%
  merge(short_topic_labels_lookup, by = "topicChoiceAsString") %>%
  merge(really_short_topic_labels_lookup, by = "topicChoiceAsString")

```

```{r}
#| label: Make focus topic dfs and grab counts for each topic
#| echo: false

# ratings of all topics
topic_ratings_df <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(phase == "pre-convo-survey-initial") %>%
  filter(question != "commitment-check") %>%
  mutate(answer = as.numeric(answer)) %>%
  select(prolific_subject_id, question, answer, topicChoiceAsString)

# ratings of just focus topic
topic_choice_rating <- topic_ratings_df %>%
  group_by(prolific_subject_id) %>%
  filter(question == topicChoiceAsString)

# counts and add short labels for ease of use later
topic_frequency_counts <- topic_choice_rating %>%
  group_by(question) %>%
  count() %>%
  rename(topicChoiceAsString = question) %>%
  merge(short_topic_labels_lookup, by = "topicChoiceAsString")
```
  
```{r}
#| label: fig-topic-ratings
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: Each subject used a 7-pt scale (where 1 meant strongly uncomfortable and 7 meant strongly comfortable) to rate how comfortable they would be discussing each of the depicted controversial topics. The topic that a subject rated lowest, meaning most uncomfortable, became the subject's focus topic for the remainder of the survey. This ridgeline plot depicts the distributions of all topic ratings. 

topic_ratings_plot <- ggplot(topic_ratings_df, aes(x = answer, y = question, fill = question)) +
  geom_density_ridges(bandwidth = 0.931) + # using the bandwidth that it automatically picks 
  # give the qualitative meanings of 1-7
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 7, by = 1)) +
   scale_y_discrete(labels = topic_labels) +
  # make it look nicer
  theme_ridges() +      
  # remove the legend
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  labs(title = "Ridgeline Plot of Ratings by Topic",
       x = "Rating (1 = Strongly uncomfortable, 7 = Strongly comfortable)",
       y = "Contentious Topic")

print(topic_ratings_plot)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-topic-ratings-only-chosen-topics
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: Each subject used a 7-pt scale (where 1 meant strongly uncomfortable and 7 meant strongly comfortable) to rate how comfortable they would be discussing each of the depicted controversial topics. The topic that a subject rated lowest, meaning most uncomfortable, became the subject's focus topic for the remainder of the survey. This ridgeline plot depicts the distributions of focus topic ratings. 

# reorder the factor levels based on n so it is easier to interpret in the graph version
topic_choice_rating <- topic_choice_rating %>%
  mutate(topicChoiceAsString = factor(topicChoiceAsString, 
                                      levels = topic_frequency_counts %>% 
                                        arrange(n) %>%  
                                        pull(topicChoiceAsString)))

# make ridgeline plot with the counts of participants in each topic at the side for easy visual 
topic_choice_ratings_plot <- ggplot(topic_choice_rating, aes(x = answer, y = topicChoiceAsString, fill = topicChoiceAsString)) +
  geom_density_ridges(bandwidth = 0.931) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(1, 7, by = 1)) +
  scale_y_discrete(labels = topic_labels) +
  theme_ridges() +      
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  labs(title = "Ridgeline Plot of Ratings by Topic",
       x = "Rating (1 = Strongly uncomfortable, 7 = Strongly comfortable)",
       y = "Contentious Topic") +
  geom_text(
    data = topic_frequency_counts,
    aes(
      x = Inf,
      y = as.factor(topicChoiceAsString),
      label = paste0("n = ", n)
    ),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    hjust = 1.1,
    size = 4
  ) +
  guides(fill = "none") +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off")

print(topic_choice_ratings_plot)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-bar-chart-topic-choice
#| include: false 
#| fig-cap: Bar chart showing the number of participants in each topic.

topic_votes_plot <- ggplot(topic_choice_rating, aes(x = answer, fill = topicChoiceAsString)) + 
  geom_bar() + 
  xlab("Willingness (1 = Strongly Unwilling, 7 = Strongly Willing)") +
  ylab("Number of participants")


topic_votes_plot
```

```{r}
#| label: Find number of participants who rated their focus topic 4 or greater
#| echo: false

topic_choice_rating_4_plus <- topic_choice_rating %>%
  filter(answer > 3)

n_subs_4_plus <- length(topic_choice_rating_4_plus$prolific_subject_id)

```


```{r}
#| label: fig-bar-topic-counts
#| echo: false 
#| fig-cap: Bar plot showing the number of participants in each topic.

topic_hist <- ggplot(topic_choice_rating, aes(x = topicChoiceAsString)) + 
  geom_bar() + 
  xlab("Focus topic") +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = really_short_topic_labels) +
  ylab("Number of participants")+
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, vjust = 1))

topic_hist
```

Our topic assignment method successfully generated a sample for whom engaging with their focus topic felt less than strongly comfortable, although not as uncomfortable as we were aiming for. Only `r round(100*(total_num_clean_subjects_in_data - n_subs_4_plus)/total_num_clean_subjects_in_data, 0)`% of subjects rated their focus topic with a rating from 1 ("Strongly uncomfortable") to 3 ("Slightly uncomfortable"), whereas `r round(100*(n_subs_4_plus/total_num_clean_subjects_in_data), 0)`% of subjects rated their focus topic with a rating from 4 ("Neutral") to 6 ("Moderately comfortable"). At a minimum, though, no participant rated their focus topic willingness at a 7 ("Strongly comfortable"), implying that every participant still experienced some level of discomfort when discussing their focus topic. \[for discussion: this could imply that people are being dishonest, that we asked the question badly, and/or that willingness is too situational and it doesn't work to ask about it in a general way\]

The participants with higher willingness ratings were not evenly distributed amongst the topics. As shown in @fig-topic-ratings-only-chosen-topics, four of the ten topics---police conduct and accountability, vaccine mandates, capitalism in economy, and climate change policy---proportionally garnered many more willingness ratings at 4 ("Neutral") or greater than the other topics. These topics were of the less popular topics (@fig-bar-topic-counts), but they still had a considerable number participants, especially capitalism in the economy and climate change policies.


As shown in @fig-bar-topic-counts, the majority of participants were least willing to discuss the role of the U.S. in the Israeli-Palestinian conflict, human euthanasia in the U.S., and transgender athletes in U.S. sports. Additionally, while deportation policy was not a popular focus topic, the ratings for this topic were lower (less willing), on average, than for the other focus topics.

```{r}
#| label: extra text i removed
#| include: false

# Specifically, `r topic_frequency_counts$n[topic_frequency_counts$topicChoiceAsString == "police conduct and accountability in the U.S."]` participants were least willing to discuss police conduct and accountability but still rated themselves as slightly to strongly comfortable discussing the topic, `r topic_frequency_counts$n[topic_frequency_counts$topicChoiceAsString == "mandating vaccines in the U.S."]` participants were least willing to discuss vaccine mandates but still rated themselves as slightly to strongly comfortable discussing the topic, `r topic_frequency_counts$n[topic_frequency_counts$topicChoiceAsString == "the role of capitalism in the U.S. economy"]` participants were least willing to discuss capitalism in economy but still rated themselves as slightly to strongly comfortable discussing the topic, and topic_frequency_counts$n[topic_frequency_counts$topicChoiceAsString == "U.S. public policy on climate change"] participants were least willing to discuss climate change but still rated themselves as slightly to stronly comfortable discussing the topic.
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-avg-willingness-by-topic
#| include: false # this is such a fugly graph
#| fig-cap: Ridgeline plot of willingness to engage in conversation about topic by topic. Participants only rated their willingness for their topic choice. Participants' willingness values were calculated through averaging the responses to the questions, "Using the slider, please rate your willingness to have a conversation with someone who strongly disagrees with you about [topic], where a score of 0 is absolute unwillingness to converse and 100 is absolute willingness to converse. A score of 50 is neutral," and "Using the slider, please rate your willingness to have a conversation with someone who strongly disagrees with you about [topic], where a score of 0 is absolute unwillingness to converse and 100 is absolute willingness to converse. A score of 50 is neutral."

#make avg willingness df by taking mean of both slides for each participant 

avg_willingness_df <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(question == "slider1-placeholder" | question == "slider2-placeholder") %>% 
  group_by(prolific_subject_id, topicChoiceAsString) %>%
  summarise(avg_willingness = mean(as.numeric(answer), na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(topicChoiceAsString = factor(topicChoiceAsString, 
                                      levels = topic_frequency_counts %>% 
                                        arrange(n) %>%  # Use arrange(desc(n)) for descending
                                        pull(topicChoiceAsString)))

#merge back with surveys_df in case i want all info in one spot in the future 
surveys_df <- avg_willingness_df %>%
  select(-topicChoiceAsString) %>%
  full_join(surveys_df, by = "prolific_subject_id")

topic_choice_ratings_plot <- ggplot(avg_willingness_df, aes(x = avg_willingness, y = topicChoiceAsString, fill = topicChoiceAsString)) +
  geom_density_ridges(bandwidth = 0.931) + # using the bandwidth that it automatically picks 
  scale_x_continuous() +
   scale_y_discrete(labels = topic_labels) +
  # make it look nicer
  theme_ridges() +      
  # remove the legend
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  labs(title = "Ridgeline Plot of Willingness to Engage by Topic",
       x = "Rating (0 = Strongly unwilling, 100 = Strongly willing)",
       y = "Contentious Topic")

topic_choice_ratings_plot

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-box-plot-willingness 
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: Distribution of average willingness ratings by topic from 0 (completely unwilling) to 100 (completely willing), where 50 is neutral. Number of participants can be found at the right edge of the figure. We asked participants to rate their willingness to discuss their focus topic with someone who strongly disagrees with them and with someone who would push back against their views, and then averaged the two results to find a participant's average willingness rating. 

#boxplot with participant counts
avg_willingness_box_plot <- ggplot(
  avg_willingness_df,
  aes(
    x = avg_willingness,
    y = topicChoiceAsString,  
    fill = topicChoiceAsString
  )
) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_text(
    data = topic_frequency_counts,
    aes(
      x = Inf,
      y = as.factor(topicChoiceAsString),
      label = paste0("n = ", n)
    ),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    hjust = 1.1,
    size = 4
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Boxplots of Willingness to Engage by Topic",
    x = "Rating (0 = Strongly unwilling, 100 = Strongly willing)",
    y = "Contentious Topic"
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(labels = topic_labels) +
  guides(fill = "none") +
  coord_cartesian(clip = "off")

avg_willingness_box_plot

```

```{r}
#| label: Find n participants who rated willingness at or above 50 
#| include: false 

avg_willingness_50_plus_df <- avg_willingness_df %>%
  filter(avg_willingness > 49)

n_avg_willingness_50_plus <- length(avg_willingness_50_plus_df$prolific_subject_id)
```

Separately from assigning focus topics, we asked participants to rate their willingness to discuss their focus topic with someone who strongly disagrees with them and with someone who would push back against their views. Participants used a slider ranging from 0 (absolute unwillingness) to 100 (absolute willingness) to respond, and we averaged the two responses to find participants' average willingness. This assessment was quite similar to that of the topic selection question; however, we asked these questions separately to ensure quality of data and to use alternative phrasing that emphasized the potential divisiveness of the discussion.

Participants' average willingness ratings, shown in @fig-box-plot-willingness, were widely spread, with a notable density of `r round(100*(n_avg_willingness_50_plus/total_num_clean_subjects_in_data), 0)`% of participants reporting scores at or above the neutral point of 50. While not completely in opposition with the Likert-scale indicator of willingness, these results suggest an even greater average willingness to converse than did the Likert-scale indicator. \[discussion: this could also reflect a situational willingness influence the likert-scale part says small group of people, whereas the average willingness ones are about one person. maybe that, or some other situational thing, made a difference to people\]

```{r}
#| label: Diff btwn two avg willingness questions 
#| echo: false 

will_df <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(question == "slider1-placeholder" | question == "slider2-placeholder") %>%
  mutate(answer = as.numeric(answer)) %>%
  select(prolific_subject_id, question, answer) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = question,
    values_from = answer
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    diff_avg_willingness = as.numeric(`slider1-placeholder`) - as.numeric(`slider2-placeholder`)
  ) %>%
  select(prolific_subject_id, "slider1-placeholder", "slider2-placeholder", diff_avg_willingness)

diff_will_df <- will_df %>%
  filter(abs(diff_avg_willingness) > 5)

n_subs_diff_will_ratings <- length(diff_will_df$prolific_subject_id)
# there are some p big differences for a handful of people, so it's good that we have both questions

```

```{r}
#| label: Data quality indicators for subs with large diffs in avg willingness
#| fig-cap: NEW FIGURE- Looks like there aren't big differences in time taken... Could look at word count of free response next? Or number of endorsed concerns? We don't have many ways of checking data quality.
#| echo: false

# attach diff_avg_willingness back to surveys_df so all data is in one df
surveys_df <- surveys_df %>%
  left_join(will_df, by = "prolific_subject_id") %>%
  rename(avg_will_disagree = "slider1-placeholder",
         avg_will_pushback = "slider2-placeholder") %>%
  mutate(diff_avg_willingness = as.numeric(diff_avg_willingness)) %>%
  mutate(time_taken_overall_minutes = as.numeric(time_taken_overall_minutes))

# look at completion times

quality_ind_willingness <- surveys_df %>%
  mutate(five_threshold = case_when(
    abs(diff_avg_willingness) > 4 ~ "different",
    .default = "same"
  )) %>%
  mutate(ten_threshold = case_when(
    abs(diff_avg_willingness) > 9 ~ "different",
    .default = "same"
  )) %>%
  mutate(interval = case_when(
    abs(diff_avg_willingness) > 9 & abs(diff_avg_willingness) < 21 ~ "ten_to_twenty",
    abs(diff_avg_willingness) > 20 & abs(diff_avg_willingness) < 31 ~ "twenty_to_thirty",
    abs(diff_avg_willingness) > 30 & abs(diff_avg_willingness) < 41 ~ "thirty_to_forty",
    abs(diff_avg_willingness) > 40 & abs(diff_avg_willingness) < 51 ~ "forty_to_fifty",
    abs(diff_avg_willingness) > 50 & abs(diff_avg_willingness) < 61 ~ "fifty_to_sixty",
    abs(diff_avg_willingness) > 60 & abs(diff_avg_willingness) < 71 ~ "sixty_to_seventy",
    abs(diff_avg_willingness) > 70 & abs(diff_avg_willingness) < 81 ~ "seventy_to_eighty",
    abs(diff_avg_willingness) > 80 & abs(diff_avg_willingness) < 91 ~ "eighty_to_ninety",
    abs(diff_avg_willingness) > 90 & abs(diff_avg_willingness) <= 100 ~ "ninety_to_hundred",
    .default = "zero_to_ten"
  )) %>%
  distinct(prolific_subject_id, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(quality_ind_willingness, aes(x = time_taken_overall_minutes, y = interval)) +
  geom_density_ridges() +
  labs(title = "Distribution of time taken on study by abs val diff in willingness Q ratings",
       x = "Time taken overall on study (minutes)",
       y = "Abs val rating difference (0 to 100)")


# look at number of concerns endorsed. this is also interesting just in general, so I'm gonna grab some distribution stats here to report on later 

n_concerns_endorsed <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(str_detect(question, "unwillingness-reason-")) %>%
  filter(phase == "willingness-concerns-first-survey") %>%
  group_by(prolific_subject_id) %>%
  count(answer == 1) %>%
  rename(n_endorsed = n) %>%
  mutate(n_endorsed = as.numeric(n_endorsed)) %>%
  filter(`answer == 1` == TRUE) %>%
  select(prolific_subject_id, n_endorsed)

sum_stats_concerns_endorsed <- n_concerns_endorsed %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  summarize(mean = round(mean(as.numeric(n_endorsed), na.rm = TRUE), 1),
            max = max(n_endorsed),
            min = min(n_endorsed),
            lower_quartile = quantile(n_endorsed, probs = 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
            upper_quartile = quantile(n_endorsed, probs = 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

surveys_df <- surveys_df %>%
  left_join(n_concerns_endorsed, by = "prolific_subject_id", relationship = "many-to-many") 

quality_ind_willingness <- quality_ind_willingness %>%
  left_join(n_concerns_endorsed, by = "prolific_subject_id") 

ggplot(quality_ind_willingness, aes(x = n_endorsed, y = interval)) +
  geom_density_ridges() +
  labs(title = "Number of concerns endorsed by abs val diff in willingness Q ratings",
       x = "Num concerns endorsed (1 to 25)",
       y = "Abs val rating difference (0 to 100)")

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-avg-willingness-diff
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: Slope plot depicting each subject's ratings of the first and second willingness questions. Each line is a subject, and the slope conveys the difference in ratings. We applied jitter to the y-axis to allow for visibility of density. The first willingness question asked participants to rate their willingness to discuss their focus topic with someone who strongly disagrees with them. The second question ask about willingness to discuss with someone who would push back against their views. 

##this plot is the most informative but it might be too confusing to readers 

will_for_plot_df <- will_df %>%
  select(-diff_avg_willingness) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c("slider1-placeholder", "slider2-placeholder"),
               names_to = "question",
               values_to = "answer") %>%
  mutate(answer = as.numeric(answer)) %>%
  mutate(has_difference = prolific_subject_id %in% diff_will_df$prolific_subject_id)


ggplot(will_for_plot_df, 
       aes(x = question, y = answer, group = prolific_subject_id)) +
  geom_line(aes(color = has_difference), alpha = 0.3, 
            position = position_jitter(width = 0, height = 2, seed = 42)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 2,
             position = position_jitter(width = 0, height = 2, seed = 42)) +
  #stat_summary(aes(group = 1), fun = mean, geom = "line", color = "red", linewidth = 1.5) + # mean line. i don't think it helps with anything really
  scale_color_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "blue", "FALSE" = "orange"),
                     labels = c("TRUE" = "Ratings are different (> 5 units)", "FALSE" = "Ratings are the same (within 5 units)"),
                     name = NULL  # remove legend title
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c("First question (disagrees)", "Second Question (pushes back)")) +
  scale_y_continuous(n.breaks = 10) +
  labs(title = "Willingness to Discuss: Disagreement vs. Pushback",
       x = "Discussion Partner Type",
       y = "Willingness Rating (0-100)") +
  theme_minimal() 

```

As seen in @fig-avg-willingness-diff, `r round(100*(n_subs_diff_will_ratings/total_num_clean_subjects_in_data), 0)`% of participants responded to the two willingness questions with ratings that differed by more than 5 units.

```{r}
#| label: fig-sliders-density
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false
#| fig-cap: Density plot displaying subjects' self-reported willingness to converse about their topic with somebody who disagrees with them and/or will challenge their views. Subjects used a scale from 0 to 100, where a rating of 0 meant subject was absolutely unwilling and a rating of 100 meant subject was absolutely willing. Subjects were asked this question in two ways; this density plot shows the mean of the two ratings. 

sliders_df <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(question == "slider1-placeholder" | question == "slider2-placeholder") %>%
  group_by(prolific_subject_id) %>%
  summarize(mean_willingness = round(mean(as.numeric(answer), na.rm = TRUE), 1))
  
sliders_density <- ggplot(sliders_df, aes(x = mean_willingness)) + #, color = condition.x)) +
  geom_density() +  
  labs(x = "Mean Willingness Rating", y = "Density of Rating", title = "Average Willingness to Converse about Selected Topic")  #, color = "Group") +
 # scale_color_manual(values = c("control free reflection" = "darkseagreen", "selection bot" = "cornflowerblue", "combination bot" = "navyblue"), 
 #                    labels = c("control free reflection" = "Control group", "selection bot" = "Experimental group: Selection bot", "combination bot" = "Experimental group: Combination bot"))

print(sliders_density)

```

```{r}
#| label: Make various short labels for concern items 
#| echo: false

concern_map <- c("Might experience negative feelings about self" = "unwillingness-reason-The conversation could negatively affect how I feel about myself during and/or afterward (e.g.,  fear, sadness, anger, vulnerability)", 
         "Might be offended" = "unwillingness-reason-I might take offense to what someone says",
  "Opinion is unpopular amongst group" = "unwillingness-reason-I would feel like my opinion is unpopular amongst the group",
  "Don't participate in these conversations often" = "unwillingness-reason-I don’t often participate in conversations like these",
  "Might struggle to explain views or seem uninformed" = "unwillingness-reason-I might struggle to explain my views to the others and/or come across as uninformed",
  "Others might be unproductive or disrespectful" = "unwillingness-reason-I would not trust the others to keep the conversation productive and respectful (e.g., keeping emotions in check, refraining from making hostile remarks, not dominating the conversation)",
  "Views wouldn't change" = "unwillingness-reason-It would be a waste of time because my views wouldn't change",
  "Don't want to listen to others" = "unwillingness-reason-I would not want to listen to the others discuss their views",
  "Might be criticized" = "unwillingness-reason-Someone could criticize or show disapproval of my views",
  "Might experience negative feelings about the world" = "unwillingness-reason-The conversation could negatively affect how I feel about the world during and/or afterward",
  "Conversation might be awkward or tense" = "unwillingness-reason-The conversation could become awkward or tense",
  "Might feel embarrassed" = "unwillingness-reason-I could feel humiliated or embarrassed in the conversation",
  "Might offend someone" = "unwillingness-reason-Someone might take offense to what I say",
  "Might struggle to remain productive and respectful" = "unwillingness-reason-I might struggle to remain productive and respectful",
  "Others won't make effort to listen" = "unwillingness-reason-The others might not make a full effort to hear and understand me",
  "Identity might be challenged" = "unwillingness-reason-Someone could challenge ideas or beliefs that play an important role in making me who I am",
  "Might feel invalidated" = "unwillingness-reason-I might feel disempowered, unheard, or invalidated",
  "Might incur social reprecussions" = "unwillingness-reason-I could incur social repercussions (e.g., being excluded by others in the future, negative changes to my reputation, feeling ostracized)",
  "Opinion is unpopular in general" = "unwillingness-reason-My opinion is unpopular in general",
  "Topic is too complex" = "unwillingness-reason-I would feel like the topic is too complex or requires more knowledge than I have",
  "No first-hand experience or relevance" = "unwillingness-reason-I don't have first-hand experience with the topic and/or it isn't relevant to me",
  "Non-social reprecussions (e.g., work)" = "unwillingness-reason-I could experience significant repercussions at work or in other spaces beyond my personal social network",
  "Too mentally draining" = "unwillingness-reason-It wouldn't be worth the emotional and/or mental energy",
  "Others' views won't change" = "unwillingness-reason-It would be a waste of time because the others' views wouldn't change",
  "Might damage relationships" = "unwillingness-reason-I would not want to hurt my relationship(s) with others or upset them"
)

# make tibble using these 
concern_lookup <- tibble(
  question = unlist(concern_map),
  short_concern_label = rep(names(concern_map), lengths(concern_map))
)

# create short labels for each question
question_key_for_fa <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(phase == "follow-up-unwillingness-concerns-survey") %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(question) %>%
  distinct(question) %>%
  arrange(question) %>%  # ensure consistent order
  mutate(short_label = paste0("Q0", row_number())) %>%
  merge(concern_lookup, by = "question")

question_key_for_printing <- question_key_for_fa %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('unwillingness-reason-', '', .))) 

```

```{r}
#| label: Most frequently endorsed concerns
#| echo: false 

percent_concerned_df <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(phase == "willingness-concerns-first-survey") %>%
    # Keep only the columns needed
    select(question, answer) %>%
    # Group by the question text
    group_by(question) %>%
    # Summarize the data for each question
    summarise(
        # Calculate the count of "Yes" answers for this question
        N_yes = sum(answer == "1", na.rm = TRUE),
        # Calculate the percentage
        percent_yes = (N_yes / n()) * 100,
        .groups = 'drop' # Ungroup after summarizing
    ) %>%
    # Select and rename the final columns
    select(
        concern = question, 
        percent_yes
    ) %>%
    # Format to 1 decimal place
    mutate(
        percent_yes = round(percent_yes, 1)
    ) %>%
    # Order the results from highest to lowest percentage
    arrange(desc(percent_yes))


# select the top 5 most frequently endorsed 
top_5_concerns <- percent_concerned_df %>%
  slice(1:5) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('unwillingness-reason-', '', .))) %>%
 pull(concern)  

```

```{r}
#| label: Most severely impacting willingness concerns 
#| echo: false

percent_severe_df <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(phase == "follow-up-unwillingness-concerns-survey") %>%
    # Keep only the columns needed
    select(question, answer) %>%
    # Group by the question text
    group_by(question) %>%
    mutate(across(everything(), ~ replace_na(.x, 1))) %>% # making the concerns that participants weren't even worried about into a 1 so they can be represented properly 
    # Summarize the data for each question
    summarise(
        # Calculate the count of "Yes" answers for this question
        N_severe = sum(answer == "7" | answer == "6", na.rm = TRUE),
        # Calculate the percentage
        percent_severe = (N_severe / n()) * 100,
        .groups = 'drop' # Ungroup after summarizing
    ) %>%
    # Select and rename the final columns
    select(
        concern = question, 
        percent_severe
    ) %>%
    # Format to 1 decimal place
    mutate(
        percent_severe = round(percent_severe, 1)
    ) %>%
    # Order the results from highest to lowest percentage
    arrange(desc(percent_severe))


# select the top 5 most frequently endorsed 
top_5_severe <- percent_severe_df %>%
  slice(1:5) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('unwillingness-reason-', '', .))) %>%
 pull(concern)  

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-openmindedness-density
#| include: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| fig-cap: Scatterplot with line of equality of pre- and post-conversation mean ratings of subjects' open-mindedness when discussing their topic. Subjects rated their open-mindedness on a scale from 0 to 7, where a rating of 0 meant subject was not open-minded and a rating of 7 meant subject was open-minded.

openmindedness_df <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(grepl("openmindedness", question)) %>%
  mutate(answer = as.numeric(answer)) 

openmindedness_summary_by_subject_df <- openmindedness_df %>%
  group_by(prolific_subject_id) %>%
 summarise(
    mean_openmindedness = round(mean(answer, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    min_openmindedness = round(min(answer, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    max_openmindedness = round(max(answer, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    median_openmindedness = round(median(answer, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
  )  %>%
  ungroup()

openmindedness_summary_df <- openmindedness_df %>%
  ungroup() %>%
 summarise(
    mean_openmindedness = round(mean(answer, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    min_openmindedness = round(min(answer, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    max_openmindedness = round(max(answer, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    median_openmindedness = round(median(answer, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
  )  %>%
  ungroup()

mean_openmindedness <- openmindedness_summary_df$mean_openmindedness
min_openmindedness <- openmindedness_summary_df$min_openmindedness
max_openmindedness <- openmindedness_summary_df$max_openmindedness
median_openmindedness <- openmindedness_summary_df$median_openmindedness


openmindedness_density <- ggplot(openmindedness_summary_df, aes(x = mean_openmindedness)) + #, color = condition.x)) +
  geom_density() +  
  labs(x = "Mean Open-mindedness Rating", y = "Density of Rating", title = "Average Open-mindedness about Selected Topic")  #, color = "Group") +
 # scale_color_manual(values = c("control free reflection" = "darkseagreen", "selection bot" = "cornflowerblue", "combination bot" = "navyblue"), 
 #                    labels = c("control free reflection" = "Control group", "selection bot" = "Experimental group: Selection bot", "combination bot" = "Experimental group: Combination bot"))

print(openmindedness_density)

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-openmindedness-likert-bar-plot
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false 
#| fig-cap: Likert bar plot displaying subjects' ratings of five questions about their open-mindedness. The five questions were derived from @elnakouri2024 and modified for application to this experiment. On a scale from 1 to 7, where a rating of 1 meant subject strongly disagreed and a rating of 7 meant strongly agree, subjects rated how true the five statements were for them. Three of the statements were negatively worded in the survey but have been worded positively and reverse-coded in this plot for clarity.

# only works though if all "1" to "7" ratings are in the df

wide_for_likert_openmindedness_df_with_sub_labels <- openmindedness_df %>%
  select(question, answer, prolific_subject_id) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = question, values_from = answer) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  rename("Has patience for opposing arguments" = "openmindedness...have little patience for arguments that I disagree with @R@", 
"Does not avoid opposing messages" = "openmindedness...avoid messages that I disagree with @R@", 
"Believes it is important to pay attention to all politics ideas" = "openmindedness...believe it is a waste of time to pay attention to certain political ideas about $topic @R@",
"Is open to other political viewpoints" = "openmindedness...am open to considering other political viewpoints about $topic",
"Considers as many different opinions as possible" = "openmindedness...consider as many different opinions as possible about $topic") 

wide_for_likert_openmindedness_df <- wide_for_likert_openmindedness_df_with_sub_labels %>%
  select(-prolific_subject_id) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ factor(.x, levels = 1:7,
     labels = c("Strongly Disagree", "Moderately Disagree", "Somewhat Disagree",
                "Neutral", "Somewhat Agree", "Moderately Agree", "Strongly Agree"),
     ordered = TRUE)))

### create a vector of the all 1-7 values. defining again here in case stuff gets moved around 

all_likert_values <- c("Strongly Disagree", "Moderately Disagree", "Somewhat Disagree", "Neutral", "Somewhat Agree", "Moderately Agree", "Strongly Agree")
### get the column names from  existing df
openmindedness_column_names <- colnames(wide_for_likert_openmindedness_df)
### create 7 new rows
new_openmindedness_likert_rows <- lapply(all_likert_values, function(response) {
  as.list(setNames(rep(response, length(openmindedness_column_names)), openmindedness_column_names))
}) %>%
  bind_rows()
### add the new rows to the existing dataframe
wide_for_likert_openmindedness_df <- bind_rows(wide_for_likert_openmindedness_df, new_openmindedness_likert_rows)


# standardize all columns to use the same factor object
wide_for_likert_openmindedness_df <- wide_for_likert_openmindedness_df %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ factor(.x, levels = all_likert_values, ordered = TRUE)))

openmindedness_likert <- likert(as.data.frame(wide_for_likert_openmindedness_df))

# for the future when comparing pre and post, might want to use this example line: df1_likert <- likert(items=df1[,3:4], grouping=df1[,2]) 

plot(openmindedness_likert, legend.position="right")
```

On average, subjects rated their open-mindedness at `r mean_openmindedness` out of 7 (Range: `r min_openmindedness`-`r max_openmindedness`), where 1 is less open-minded and 7 is more open-minded, as observed in @fig-openmindedness-likert-bar-plot. Given later findings on the top concerns that people have, it is notable here that "Does not avoid opposing messages" has the lowest open-mindedness ratings from participants. 

```{r}
#| label: Making free response csv for chat analysis 
#| echo: false

free_response_df <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(question == "willingness-free-response") %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(prolific_subject_id, answer, topicChoiceAsString, Age, Sex, "Ethnicity simplified", "Employment status") %>%
  rename(
    #Condition = condition,
    'Focus topic' = 'topicChoiceAsString',
    'Free response' = 'answer'
    )

write.csv(free_response_df, "rep_sample_free_response_data.csv", row.names = FALSE)
```

```{r}
#| label: Df for 28 manually reviewed free responses 
#| echo: false 

# I used random.org to randomly select 28 numbers from 1 to 284, which will correspond to the row number in the free_response df

# 1. 5
# 2. 69
# 3. 131
# 4. 86
# 5. 134
# 6. 214
# 7. 240
# 8. 42
# 9. 94
# 10. 276
# 11. 187
# 12. 29
# 13. 171
# 14. 55
# 15. 184
# 16. 49
# 17. 201
# 18. 165
# 19. 176
# 20. 14
# 21. 216
# 22. 283
# 23. 1
# 24. 103
# 25. 153
# 26. 218
# 27. 284
# 28. 252

free_response_manual_df <- free_response_df %>%
  filter(prolific_subject_id == "564dd73edc21cd000dba8ce1" |
           prolific_subject_id == "610a151c96de07b15f28c8cb" |
           prolific_subject_id == "662fecef2a008847dc4f0d9b" |
           prolific_subject_id == "631c8e97db06f601f81bd82f" |
           prolific_subject_id == "6631997e0554bb6d25062fd4" |
           prolific_subject_id == "67d6ffe7dad4354b208e186d" |
           prolific_subject_id == "694f4d4cf7f1d3401de99e61" |
           prolific_subject_id == "5e0bd25e67f1703eafedd160" |
           prolific_subject_id == "63fb73b8e64f455117b045b9" |
           prolific_subject_id == "697cd31d69677a82b9e94fb3" |
           prolific_subject_id == "673f58421b143a15bc2367c1" |
           prolific_subject_id == "5cab69f77ea4a700019a87dc" |
           prolific_subject_id == "66e9d794bd67a9a02ca66a8f" |
           prolific_subject_id == "5fc07d1d954a2251529dcfd6" |
           prolific_subject_id == "5ed559e5afbd830e2117ed55" |
           prolific_subject_id == "67364113bdafe2840fa05b69" |
           prolific_subject_id == "678041088952b9da0a4734ae" |
           prolific_subject_id == "66ccd41993bcfa83410adbf0" |
           prolific_subject_id == "670d2af9c00f086b201e9f70" |
           prolific_subject_id == "5a4ff86beedc32000141f232" |
           prolific_subject_id == "67e8aa454848956e5e32c19d" |
           prolific_subject_id == "697d4f4c2229572d1e5d26a3" |
           prolific_subject_id == "5563984afdf99b672b5749b6" |
           prolific_subject_id == "64bbf16db125b5d6a2f3a911" |
           prolific_subject_id == "66779b54dfca48b1e171a63b" |
           prolific_subject_id == "67ebc24ee98e314102e23f1a" |
           prolific_subject_id == "697d5370cf685ff023402820" |
           prolific_subject_id == "696125abb83cbbfa966d0e64"
           )

```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-free-response-examples-table
#| include: false
#| tbl-cap: Table showing examples of uncaptured concerns in subjects' responses to free response question, which asked subjects "Think back to the last time you chose to not participate in a conversation about [topic] or another topic you found uncomfortable to discuss. In the space below, please describe why you decided to not engage. Consider whether your reasons align with any of the potential concerns listed in this survey, or if you had different concerns."

# This table should show a couple very typical responses, which, based on LLM analysis, should probably be consistent with our nineteen items 

# free_response_for_table <- free_response_df %>%
#    filter(prolific_subject_id == "5d3695969749cc00165c222f"
#           | prolific_subject_id == "66293ab7b323f5f7ce785c38"
#           | prolific_subject_id == "60712d937752fb8780e89951"
#           | prolific_subject_id == "61074ae762b0186a302f1028"
#           | prolific_subject_id == "565bff58c121fe0005fc390d"
#           | prolific_subject_id == "666efed91a31f3c82021f11d") %>%
#    select("Free response", "Focus topic")

# kable(free_response_for_table, "html") %>%
#   kable_styling() %>%
#   column_spec(2, width = "5em")
```

### Comprehensiveness of Concerns


```{r}
#| label: fig-unwillingness-reason-likert-bar-plot
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: Likert bar plot displaying subjects' ratings of how much certain concerns contribute to their unwillingness to converse. The 25 potential concerns were derived from existing literature and pretested in a pilot experiment. On a scale from 1 to 7, where a rating of 1 meant participant's willingness is not at all impacted by the concern and a rating of 7 meant participant's willingness is strongly impacted by the concern, participants rated the potential concerns' effects on their willingness to converse.

# only works though if all "1" to "7" ratings are in the df

wide_unwill_reasons_df_1 <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(grepl("unwillingness-reason", question)) %>%
  filter(phase == "follow-up-unwillingness-concerns-survey") %>%
  select(question, answer, prolific_subject_id) %>%
  group_by(prolific_subject_id) %>%
  mutate(answer = as.numeric(answer)) %>%
  pivot_wider(id_cols = prolific_subject_id, names_from = question, values_from = answer) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  rename("Might experience negative feelings about self" = "unwillingness-reason-The conversation could negatively affect how I feel about myself during and/or afterward (e.g.,  fear, sadness, anger, vulnerability)", 
         "Might be offended" = "unwillingness-reason-I might take offense to what someone says",
  "Opinion is unpopular amongst group" = "unwillingness-reason-I would feel like my opinion is unpopular amongst the group",
  "Don't participate in these conversations often" = "unwillingness-reason-I don’t often participate in conversations like these",
  "Might struggle to explain views or seem uninformed" = "unwillingness-reason-I might struggle to explain my views to the others and/or come across as uninformed",
  "Others might be unproductive or disrespectful" = "unwillingness-reason-I would not trust the others to keep the conversation productive and respectful (e.g., keeping emotions in check, refraining from making hostile remarks, not dominating the conversation)",
  "Views wouldn't change" = "unwillingness-reason-It would be a waste of time because my views wouldn't change",
  "Don't want to listen to others" = "unwillingness-reason-I would not want to listen to the others discuss their views",
  "Might be criticized" = "unwillingness-reason-Someone could criticize or show disapproval of my views",
  "Might experience negative feelings about the world" = "unwillingness-reason-The conversation could negatively affect how I feel about the world during and/or afterward",
  "Conversation might be awkward or tense" = "unwillingness-reason-The conversation could become awkward or tense",
  "Might feel embarrassed" = "unwillingness-reason-I could feel humiliated or embarrassed in the conversation",
  "Might offend someone" = "unwillingness-reason-Someone might take offense to what I say",
  "Might struggle to remain productive and respectful" = "unwillingness-reason-I might struggle to remain productive and respectful",
  "Others won't make effort to listen" = "unwillingness-reason-The others might not make a full effort to hear and understand me",
  "Identity might be challenged" = "unwillingness-reason-Someone could challenge ideas or beliefs that play an important role in making me who I am",
  "Might feel invalidated" = "unwillingness-reason-I might feel disempowered, unheard, or invalidated",
  "Might incur social reprecussions" = "unwillingness-reason-I could incur social repercussions (e.g., being excluded by others in the future, negative changes to my reputation, feeling ostracized)",
  "Opinion is unpopular in general" = "unwillingness-reason-My opinion is unpopular in general",
  "Topic is too complex" = "unwillingness-reason-I would feel like the topic is too complex or requires more knowledge than I have",
  "No first-hand experience or relevance" = "unwillingness-reason-I don't have first-hand experience with the topic and/or it isn't relevant to me",
  "Non-social reprecussions (e.g., work)" = "unwillingness-reason-I could experience significant repercussions at work or in other spaces beyond my personal social network",
  "Too mentally draining" = "unwillingness-reason-It wouldn't be worth the emotional and/or mental energy",
  "Others' views won't change" = "unwillingness-reason-It would be a waste of time because the others' views wouldn't change",
  "Might damage relationships" = "unwillingness-reason-I would not want to hurt my relationship(s) with others or upset them"
) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ replace_na(.x, 1))) # making the concerns that participants weren't even worried about into a 1 so that they can be represented in the likert graph as "not at all"


wide_unwill_reasons_df_2 <- wide_unwill_reasons_df_1 %>%
  select(-prolific_subject_id) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ factor(.x, levels = 1:7,
     labels = c("Not at all", "Slightly", "Somewhat",
                "Moderately", "Quite a bit", "Very much", "Extremely"),
     ordered = TRUE))) 

unwillingness_reason_likert <- likert(as.data.frame(wide_unwill_reasons_df_2))

# for the future when comparing pre and post, might want to use this example line: df1_likert <- likert(items=df1[,3:4], grouping=df1[,2])

plot(
  unwillingness_reason_likert, 
  legend.position="right",
  colors = c("#63666A", "#9A9B9C", "#D0D0CE", "#FFF8EF", "#F2D4D7", "#B2182B", "#641A2B")) +
  labs(title = "What Concerns Deter Americans from Engaging in Difficult Conversations?") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(
      hjust = 0.5,      # center
      size = 10         # font size
    )
  ) 

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-unwillingness-reason-only-endorsed-likert-bar-plot
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: Likert bar plot displaying subjects' ratings of how much certain concerns contribute to their unwillingness to converse. The nineteen potential concerns were derived from existing literature and pretested in a pilot experiment. On a scale from 1 to 7, where a rating of 1 meant participant's willingness is not at all impacted by the concern and a rating of 7 meant participant's willingness is strongly impacted by the concern, participants rated the potential concerns' effects on their willingness to converse. This plot only shows ratings of 2 and greater to display the distributions of concern severity when a concern does impact willingness.

wide_unwill_reasons_df_3 <- wide_unwill_reasons_df_2 %>%
 mutate(across(everything(), ~ na_if(.,"Not at all")))

  
unwillingness_reason_only_endorsed_likert <- likert(as.data.frame(wide_unwill_reasons_df_3))

# for the future when comparing pre and post, might want to use this example line: df1_likert <- likert(items=df1[,3:4], grouping=df1[,2])

suppressWarnings(plot(unwillingness_reason_only_endorsed_likert, legend.position="right"))

```

On average, subjects endorsed `r round(sum_stats_concerns_endorsed$mean, 0)` concerns as impacting their willingness at all (Min = `r sum_stats_concerns_endorsed$min`, LQ = `r sum_stats_concerns_endorsed$lower_quartile`, UQ = `r sum_stats_concerns_endorsed$upper_quartile`, Max = `r sum_stats_concerns_endorsed$max`).

The top five most frequently endorsed concerns (answered "Yes") were: `r paste0(shQuote(top_5_concerns[1:5]), collapse=", ")`.

The top five concerns most severely impacting willingness were `r paste0(shQuote(top_5_severe[1:5]), collapse=", ")`. The full distribution of severity ratings can be observed in @fig-unwillingness-reason-likert-bar-plot.

### Factor Analysis on Concerns

We examined the initial binary endorsement ratings of our concern items where each participant simply indicated whether an item concerned them at all, as well as the Likert-scale ratings of how much each of the endorsed items impact the participant's willingness.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-free-response
#| include: false
#| fig-cap: Table of example free responses in which participants expressed avoidance of conversations because they felt that the topic was too complex or that they did not know enough about the topic to engage in a conversation about it.


# fr_knowledge <- free_response_df %>%
#   filter(prolific_subject_id == "67ac21373a76570ef0a5c0d3" | prolific_subject_id == "66b979589d208d3ec691d89b" | prolific_subject_id == "5d3695969749cc00165c222f") %>%
#   select("Free response", "Focus topic")

# kable(fr_knowledge, "html") %>%
#   kable_styling() %>%
#   column_spec(2, width = "5em")

```

```{r}
#| label: Wrangle data for factor analysis
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| echo: false

# join short labels back to the main data
surveys_df <- surveys_df %>%
  full_join(question_key_for_fa, by = "question")

concerns_labeled_wide <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(phase == "follow-up-unwillingness-concerns-survey") %>%
  select(prolific_subject_id, short_label, answer) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = short_label, values_from = answer) %>%
  mutate(across(starts_with("Q0"), ~ as.numeric(as.character(.)))) %>%
  ungroup() 

concerns_labeled_wide_na_replaced <- concerns_labeled_wide %>%
   mutate(across(everything(), ~ replace_na(.x, 1))) # making the concerns that participants weren't even worried about into a 1 so that they can be represented in the likert graph as "not at all"

concerns_wide_na_replaced <- concerns_labeled_wide_na_replaced %>%
  select(-prolific_subject_id)

concerns_wide <- concerns_labeled_wide %>%
  select(-prolific_subject_id)

row_na_counts <- rowSums(is.na(concerns_labeled_wide))
total_na <- sum(row_na_counts)
avg_na_per_row <- mean(row_na_counts)

```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-correlation-matrix
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: Correlation matrix of reasons for unwillingness

cor_matrix <- cor(concerns_wide_na_replaced, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

library(corrplot)
corrplot <- corrplot(cor_matrix, method = "color", type = "upper", tl.cex = 0.7)

# the items all seem to show low to moderate correlations. pretty solid
```

```{r}
#| label: Check KMO, Bartlett's test, and Rit values to see if data is well-suited for factor analysis 
#| include: false 

# KMO and Bartlett’s test
kmo <- KMO(concerns_wide_na_replaced)
cortest_bartlett <- cortest.bartlett(cor(concerns_wide_na_replaced), n = nrow(concerns_wide_na_replaced))

#overall msa is above 0.8 so our data is well-suited for factor analysis!  

#cortest_bartlett shows that the p-value is VERY small, so correlations are statistically significant 

#check internal consistency
rit.vals <- alpha(concerns_wide_na_replaced)

# all indicators suggest that the data is well-suited for factor analysis 

```

```{r}
#| label: Factor analysis on 25 items 
#| include: false

# determine number of factors with parallel analysis. this will output a suggested number of factors and a scree plot 
fa_parallel_results <- fa.parallel(concerns_wide_na_replaced, fa = "fa")

# the results of parallel analysis suggest 4 factors 

# run factor analysis with 4 factors 
## we use oblimin because the factors are probably correlated 
fa_result <- fa(
    concerns_wide_na_replaced,
    nfactors = 4,
    rotate = "oblimin"
    ) 

#get explained variance for report 
total_var_explained_percent <- round(sum(fa_result$Vaccounted["Proportion Var", ]) * 100, 1)

#get factor correlations 
factor_corrs <- fa_result$r 

# extract min and max off-diagonal correlations
cor_range <- range(factor_corrs[lower.tri(factor_corrs)])
cor_min <- round(cor_range[1], 2)
cor_max <- round(cor_range[2], 2)
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-factor-loadings-visual
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: Table visualizing factor loadings in exploratory factor analysis.

library(tidyverse)

# visualize factor loadings
#prepping dfs
loadings_full_df <- as.data.frame(fa_result$loadings[1:25, ]) %>%
  rename("1" = "MR1",
         "2" = "MR2",
         "3" = "MR3",
         "4" = "MR4")

loadings_full_df$short_label <- rownames(loadings_full_df)

#pivot for readability
loadings_full_long <- loadings_full_df %>% 
  pivot_longer(-short_label, names_to = "factor", values_to = "loading")

# make digestible df with only the top loading items so we can see which concerns are in each factor
loadings_max_df <- loadings_full_long %>%
  group_by(short_label) %>%
  slice_max(order_by = abs(loading), n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  merge(question_key_for_fa, by = "short_label") %>%
  arrange(factor)

#make plot for visualization 
loadings_full_long <- loadings_full_long %>%
  merge(question_key_for_fa, by = "short_label")

factor_loadings_plot <- ggplot(loadings_full_long, aes(x = reorder(short_concern_label, loading), y = loading, fill = factor)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Factor Loadings",
    x = "Concern",
    y = "Loading",
    fill = "Factor"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

#pull items for printing later
factor_1_items <- loadings_max_df %>%
  filter(factor == "1") %>%
 pull(short_concern_label)  

factor_2_items <- loadings_max_df %>%
  filter(factor == "2") %>%
 pull(short_concern_label)  

factor_3_items <- loadings_max_df %>%
  filter(factor == "3") %>%
 pull(short_concern_label)  

factor_4_items <- loadings_max_df %>%
  filter(factor == "4") %>%
 pull(short_concern_label)  

#make item order for printing
item_order <- loadings_max_df %>%
  arrange(factor, desc(loading)) %>%
  pull(short_concern_label)

# print both ways
factor_loadings_plot

# use gt lib for pretty printing
loadings_full_long %>% 
  select(factor, loading, short_concern_label) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = factor, values_from = loading) %>%
  arrange(match(short_concern_label, item_order)) %>%
  gt() %>%
  cols_label(short_concern_label = "Concern") %>%
  fmt_number(decimals = 2) %>%
  data_color(
    columns = where(is.numeric),
    method = "numeric",
    palette = c("blue", "white", "red"),
    domain= c(-1, 1)
  )

```

```{r}
#| label: characterizing factor scores 
#| echo: false

# make factor scores into a df and match up with subject id's from the df i used to run factor analysis
factor_scores_df <- as.data.frame(fa_result$scores) %>%
  mutate(prolific_subject_id = concerns_labeled_wide_na_replaced$prolific_subject_id)

# now summarize factor scores across sample
standardized_summary_factor_scores_df <- factor_scores_df %>%
  summarise(across(starts_with("MR"), list(mean = mean, sd = sd), na.rm = TRUE))

super_summary_factor_scores_df <- factor_scores_df %>%
  pivot_longer(starts_with("MR"), names_to = "factor", values_to = "score") %>%
  group_by(prolific_subject_id) %>%
  slice_max(score, n = 1) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  count(factor) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / n_distinct(factor_scores_df$prolific_subject_id))

```

```{r}
#| label: characterize sample esndorsement and ratings of factors 
#| echo: false

## data prep
#merge with big df 
surveys_df <- surveys_df %>%
  left_join(loadings_max_df %>% select(question, factor), by = "question")

# separate out endorsement and severity from the answer column
endorsements <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(phase == "willingness-concerns-first-survey") %>%
  select(prolific_subject_id, question, endorsed = answer)

severity <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(phase == "follow-up-unwillingness-concerns-survey") %>%
  select(prolific_subject_id, question, severity = answer)


# now count number of items in each factor 
factor_endorsement_summary <- endorsements %>%
  left_join(loadings_max_df %>% select(question, factor), by = "question") %>%
  filter(endorsed == 1) %>%  # 1 means yes, 2 means no
  group_by(prolific_subject_id, factor) %>%
  summarise(n_endorsed = n()) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  group_by(factor) %>%
  summarise(
    pct_subjects_endorsing_any = n_distinct(prolific_subject_id) / n_distinct(endorsements$prolific_subject_id),
    mean_items_endorsed = mean(n_endorsed)
  ) %>%
  mutate(n_items_in_factor = case_when(
    factor == "1" ~ length(factor_1_items),
    factor == "2" ~ length(factor_2_items),
    factor == "3" ~ length(factor_3_items),
    factor == "4" ~ length(factor_4_items),
    .default = NA
  ))

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-fa-heat-map-df
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: Table showing heat map of factor analysis on concern items. 

fa_heat_map <- ggplot(loadings_full_long, aes(
  x = factor, 
  y = factor(short_concern_label, levels = item_order), 
  fill = loading)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "blue", mid = "white", high = "red",
    midpoint = 0, limit = c(-1, 1), name = "Loading"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Heat Map of Factor Loadings",
    x = "Factor",
    y = "Concern"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10))

print(fa_heat_map)

```

We conducted an exploratory factor analysis (EFA) on the concern items using oblimin rotation to identify latent factors that could reflect correlated concerns. Parallel analysis suggested a four-factor solution, which accounted for `r total_var_explained_percent`% of the total variance. The factors were moderately correlated ($r$ = `r cor_min`–`r cor_max`) as shown in @tbl-correlation-matrix. Many cross-loadings were present, suggesting some conceptual overlap among factors. We retained all items to allow full examination of the emerging structure.

If these factors reveal true overarching themes in the concern items, we interpret them as:

Social & Reputation Consequences (Factor 1): This factor reflects fears of external, social consequences. The items in this factor are: `r paste0(shQuote(factor_1_items), collapse=", ")`.

Perceived Futility & Emotional Cost (Factor 2): This factor reflects a sense that the conversation is not worth engaging in. The items in this factor are: `r paste0(shQuote(factor_2_items), collapse=", ")`.

Distrust of Conversational Process (Factor 3): This factor captures doubts of conversational efficacy and productivity. The items in this factor are: `r paste0(shQuote(factor_3_items), collapse=", ")`.

Personal Vulnerability & Identity Threat (Factor 4): This first factor consists of items focusing on internal psychological distress and threats to the participant's self-concept or core beliefs. The items in this factor are: `r paste0(shQuote(factor_4_items), collapse=", ")`.

`r round(100*(factor_endorsement_summary$pct_subjects_endorsing_any[factor_endorsement_summary$factor == "1"]), 0)` percent of subjects endorsed at least one concern in factor 1. Of the `r factor_endorsement_summary$n_items_in_factor[factor_endorsement_summary$factor == "1"]` items in factor 1, subjects endorsed an average of `r factor_endorsement_summary$mean_items_endorsed[factor_endorsement_summary$factor == "1"]` items. Similarly, `r round(100*(factor_endorsement_summary$pct_subjects_endorsing_any[factor_endorsement_summary$factor == "2"]), 0)` percent endorsed at least one concern in factor 2. Of the `r factor_endorsement_summary$n_items_in_factor[factor_endorsement_summary$factor == "2"]` items in factor 1, subjects endorsed an average of `r factor_endorsement_summary$mean_items_endorsed[factor_endorsement_summary$factor == "2"]` items. `r round(100*(factor_endorsement_summary$pct_subjects_endorsing_any[factor_endorsement_summary$factor == "3"]), 0)` endorsed at least one concern in factor 3. Of the `r factor_endorsement_summary$n_items_in_factor[factor_endorsement_summary$factor == "3"]` items in factor 1, subjects endorsed an average of `r factor_endorsement_summary$mean_items_endorsed[factor_endorsement_summary$factor == "3"]` items. Finally, `r round(100*(factor_endorsement_summary$pct_subjects_endorsing_any[factor_endorsement_summary$factor == "4"]), 0)` endorsed at least one concern in factor 4. Of the `r factor_endorsement_summary$n_items_in_factor[factor_endorsement_summary$factor == "4"]` items in factor 1, subjects endorsed an average of `r factor_endorsement_summary$mean_items_endorsed[factor_endorsement_summary$factor == "4"]` items.

Factor scores were computed using the regression method. Factor 1 was the dominant concern domain for `r round(100*(super_summary_factor_scores_df$pct[super_summary_factor_scores_df$factor == "1"]), 0)` percent of participants. Factor 2 was the dominant concern domain for `r round(100*(super_summary_factor_scores_df$pct[super_summary_factor_scores_df$factor == "2"]), 0)` percent of participants. Factor 3 was the dominant concern domain for `r round(100*(super_summary_factor_scores_df$pct[super_summary_factor_scores_df$factor == "3"]), 0)` percent of participants. Factor 4 was the dominant concern domain for `r round(100*(super_summary_factor_scores_df$pct[super_summary_factor_scores_df$factor == "4"]), 0)` percent of participants.


### Participant concern profiles (PCA and cluster analysis)

```{r}
#| label: PCA for profiling participants
#| include: false

# run pca on participants 
pca_result <- prcomp(concerns_wide_na_replaced, scale. = TRUE)
summary(pca_result)

#screeplot helps search for an elbow that indicates where the number of principal components should be cut off
screeplot(pca_result, type = "lines")

#biplot is hard to gain info from with this many observations and vectors 
biplot(pca_result, scale = 0)

## look at proportions of variance explained by each principal component 
pve <- summary(pca_result)[["importance"]]["Proportion of Variance",]
#plot
plot(1:length(pve), pve, ylim = c(0,1), xlab = "PC", 
     ylab = "Prop Variance Explained", type = "b", pch = 20)

## i find cumulative more helpful:
cumpve <- summary(pca_result)[["importance"]]["Cumulative Proportion",]
cumpve
#plot
plot(1:length(cumpve), cumpve, ylim = c(0,1), xlab = "PC", 
     ylab = "Cum Prop Variance Explained", type = "b", pch = 20)

# add pca_results to the og concerns df
concerns_labeled_wide_na_replaced <- data.frame(concerns_labeled_wide_na_replaced, pca_result$x)

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-pca-scree-plot
#| fig-cap: Scree plot indicating some structure in the principal components--- meaningful drop from PC1 to PC2, and more notable elbow from PC4 to PC5. Then all components are relatively negligible contributors. Suggests we should use 4 PCs.
#| echo: false 

screeplot(pca_result, type = "lines")
```

```{r}
#| label: look at pca loadings
#| echo: false

# grab four pc loadings 
four_pca_loadings <- as.data.frame(round(pca_result$rotation[, 1:4], 2)) %>%
  arrange(desc(PC2)) %>%
  mutate(PC1 = as.numeric(PC1),
         PC2 = as.numeric(PC2),
         PC3 = as.numeric(PC3),
         PC4 = as.numeric(PC4))

#switch out labels for the shortened text labels
```
