```{r}
rownames(four_pca_loadings) <- question_key_for_fa$short_concern_label[match(rownames(four_pca_loadings), question_key_for_fa$short_label)]

# highlight the extreme loadings so i can pay closer attention 
extreme_pc1_loadings <- four_pca_loadings %>%
  filter(abs(PC1) > 0.27) %>%
  select(PC1)

extreme_pc2_loadings <- four_pca_loadings %>%
  filter(abs(PC2) > 0.27) %>%
  select(PC2)

extreme_pc3_loadings <- four_pca_loadings %>%
  filter(abs(PC3) > 0.27) %>%
  select(PC3)

extreme_pc4_loadings <- four_pca_loadings %>%
  filter(abs(PC4) > 0.27) %>%
  select(PC4)
```

We conducted a principal component analysis (PCA) on participants' concern rating profiles to examine the dimensionality of their responses. The scree plot (@fig-pca-scree-plot) indicated that four principal components captured the majority of explainable variance, and we focus our interpretation on these.

The first principal component, accounting for `r round(100*(pve[1]), 0)`% of variance, showed uniformly positive loadings across all items (range: 0.10–0.27). This pattern is consistent with a general response factor: participants who score high on PC1 rate all concerns highly, reflecting an overall tendency toward greater reluctance rather than concern about any particular aspect of conversation.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-pca-two-loadings 
#| fig-cap: Table displaying the concern items with loadings greater than 0.27 or less than -0.27 for the second principal component

kable(extreme_pc2_loadings, caption = "Concern items with extreme PC2 loadings")
```

The second principal component accounted for `r round(100*(pve[2]), 0)`% of variance. Its highest-loading items reflect concerns about the futility of engagement and the anticipated emotional or mental costs outweighing potential benefits. This pattern is consistent with the broader sentiment observed in @fig-unwillingness-reason-likert-bar-plot.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-pca-three-loadings 
#| fig-cap: Table displaying the concern items with loadings greater than 0.27 or less than -0.27 for the third principal component

kable(extreme_pc3_loadings, caption = "Concern items with extreme PC3 loadings")
```

The third principal component accounted for `r round(100*pve[3], 0)`% of variance and contrasted two distinct sources of reluctance. Negatively loading items reflected concern about holding an unpopular opinion and the social consequences that might follow from expressing it. Positively loading items reflected a sense of being ill-equipped to engage due to topic complexity, limited first-hand experience, or difficulty articulating one's views.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-pca-four-loadings 
#| fig-cap: Table displaying the concern items with loadings greater than 0.27 or less than -0.27 for the fourth principal component

kable(extreme_pc4_loadings, caption = "Concern items with extreme PC4 loadings")
```

The fourth principal component accounted for `r round(100*pve[4], 0)`% of variance and captured a similar but distinct contrast. Negatively loading items reflected reluctance stemming from unfamiliarity with the topic and with navigating disagreement. Positively loading items reflected avoidance motivated by anticipated negative affect, both self-directed and directed toward others.

```{r}
#| label: Hierarchical cluster analysis
#| include: false

#calculate distances
concern_dists <- concerns_wide_na_replaced %>%
  dist()

#perform the clustering with average and complete linkage 
hclustmodaverage <- concern_dists %>% hclust(method = "average")
hclustmodcomplete <- concern_dists %>% hclust(method = "complete")

#plot full dendrograms
plot(hclustmodaverage)
plot(hclustmodcomplete)

#moving forward with complete linkage tree because it is more distributed than average linkage

plot(hclustmodcomplete) %>%
abline(h = 24, col = "blue", lty = 2)

# cutting at 3 because it is as even as i can get the clusters to be
completecut4 <- cutree(hclustmodcomplete, k = 4)
table(completecut4)

# add cluster id back to the df 
concerns_with_cluster_df <- concerns_labeled_wide_na_replaced %>% mutate(clusterID = completecut4) 

#get percentages to see the distribution amongst the clusters 
hierarchical_cluster_percentages <- concerns_with_cluster_df %>%
  group_by(clusterID) %>%                       # group by cluster
  summarise(n = n()) %>%  # count unique subjects
  mutate(percent = round(n / sum(n) * 100, 1))       # calculate percentage

# add to surveys_df
hierarchical_cluster_info <- concerns_with_cluster_df %>%
  select(prolific_subject_id, clusterID)

surveys_df <- surveys_df %>%
  left_join(hierarchical_cluster_info, by = "prolific_subject_id")

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-dendrogram-hierarchical-clustering
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: A dendrogram cut at four clusters shows the results of hierarchical cluster analysis using complete linkage and Euclidean distance to look for common concern profiles amongst participants. 

# print the dendrogram with the cut that i selected in previous code chunk 
plot(hclustmodcomplete) %>%
abline(h = 22, col = "blue", lty = 2)

```

```{r}
#| label: heat-map-hierarchical-clustering-on-items
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: Heat map of each cluster's average rating of each potential concern per hierarchical cluster analysis

#make df for heatmap 
hierarchical_for_heatmap_df <- concerns_with_cluster_df %>%
  group_by(clusterID) %>%
  summarise(across(starts_with("Q"), mean, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(Q01,Q02,Q03,Q04,Q05,Q06,Q07,Q08,Q09,Q010,Q011,Q012,Q013,Q014,Q015,Q016,Q017,Q018,Q019,Q020,Q021,Q022,Q023,Q024,Q025), names_to = "short_label", values_to = "mean_rating") %>%
  merge(question_key_for_fa, by = "short_label")

#get pc1 order to sort clusters in heatmap 
heat_map_concern_order <- hierarchical_for_heatmap_df %>%
  filter(clusterID == 4) %>%
  arrange(mean_rating) %>%
  pull(short_concern_label)

#make heat map to see what the cluster profiles actually look like  
hierarchical_clustering_heatmap <- ggplot(hierarchical_for_heatmap_df, 
    aes(x = factor(clusterID), y = factor(short_concern_label, levels = heat_map_concern_order), fill = mean_rating)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#FFFFFF", high = "#A6192E") + 
  labs(x = "Participant Profile", y = "Concern", fill = "Mean Rating", title = "Four Distinct Profiles of Conversational Reluctance") +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8))  # no more angle needed

#print heat map and the question key for richer understanding. if i actually include these in the final print, i'll need to add some gridExtra packaging here to make them be side by side
hierarchical_clustering_heatmap

# look at pc scores by cluster
 pc_scores <- as.data.frame(pca_result$x[, 1:4])
 pc_scores$clusterID <- concerns_with_cluster_df$clusterID
 
 # this is printable
pc_scores_by_cluster <- pc_scores %>%
   group_by(clusterID) %>%
   summarise(across(everything(), mean))

```


```{r}
#| label: fig-pca-cluster-scatterplot
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: Plot displaying participant clusters by their scores on the first and second principal components found through principal component analysis (PCA). We used hierarchical cluster analysis with Euclidean distance and complete linkage to create four clusters of participants based on their concern profile. The first principal component is likely a general factor, where participants score higher on this component if they generally rated the 25 concern items higher. The second principal component appears to differentiate between high scorers who are concerned about the futility of engagement and the anticipated emotional or mental costs outweighing potential benefits, and others who scored moderately.

pc_scores_df <- as.data.frame(pca_result$x[, 1:2])
pc_scores_df$clusterID <- factor(concerns_with_cluster_df$clusterID)

ggplot(pc_scores_df, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = clusterID)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, size = 2) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Cluster") +
  labs(
    title = "Participant Concern Profiles in PC Space",
    x = "PC1 (Overall Concern Level)",
    y = "PC2 (Futility vs. Social Concerns)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

```

We also conducted hierarchical clustering (euclidean distance, complete linkage), on participants' ratings of concerns to determine whether there are trends in participants ratings that could form general participant concern profiles. Analysis identified four distinct participant profiles based on their concern ratings.

The majority of participants, `r hierarchical_cluster_percentages$percent[hierarchical_cluster_percentages$clusterID == 1]`%, fell into Cluster 1, reporting uniformly low concern across all items. This suggests that most participants in the sample self-reported as being broadly willing to engage in difficult conversations without meaningful reluctance toward any particular aspect. A further `r hierarchical_cluster_percentages$percent[hierarchical_cluster_percentages$clusterID == 2]`% fell into Cluster 2, meaning that nearly nine in ten participants reported relatively low overall concern. Cluster 2 participants did show a relative elevation on items related to holding an unpopular opinion, suggesting that even among the less-reluctant majority, social image concerns represent the most salient source of hesitation when it does arise.

Cluster 3 participants, `r hierarchical_cluster_percentages$percent[hierarchical_cluster_percentages$clusterID == 3]`% of the sample, reported moderate-to-high concern broadly with particular elevation on items reflecting a sense of being ill-equipped to engage. This included struggling to explain their views, feeling invalidated, and finding the topic too complex. Their reluctance appears driven primarily by perceived competence and capacity constraints rather than anticipated social consequences.

Cluster 4 participants reported the highest concern overall, with especially strong ratings on items reflecting anticipated futility: that the conversation would be too mentally draining, that others would not make an effort to listen, and that neither their own nor others' views would change as a result. Unlike Cluster 3, whose reluctance is self-directed, Cluster 4's reluctance is directed outward toward skepticism about the value and likely outcome of engagement itself. Just `r hierarchical_cluster_percentages$percent[hierarchical_cluster_percentages$clusterID == 4]`% of participants fell into Cluster 4.

Taken together, the four clusters suggest a structure organized along two dimensions: overall level of reluctance, and, among those with higher reluctance, whether that reluctance is rooted in concerns about one's own capacity to engage or in doubts about whether engagement would be worthwhile.

These findings should be interpreted in light of the methodological consideration that although participants rated all 25 concern items, on average, only `r round(sum_stats_concerns_endorsed$mean, 0)` concerns actually affected a given participant's willingness to engage. Rating concerns that were personally irrelevant may have deflated mean ratings across the board [not sure whether this is actually valid for me to say, but i feel like all the 1 ratings brought everything down], potentially pushing participants toward the lower-concern clusters (??). The low ratings in Clusters 1 and 2 may therefore reflect not an absence of reluctance per se, but rather that most participants' reluctance is relatively selective. Cluster 2's relative elevation on unpopular opinion items is consistent with this interpretation: even among the less-reluctant majority, social image concerns represent the most salient source of hesitation when it does arise.


```{r}
#| label: extra text 
#| include: false

#Cluster 1's average rating of a given concern was `r cluster_means$overall_mean[cluster_means$cluster == 1]`. On average, Cluster 1 rated the following concerns the highest: `r paste(shQuote(cluster1_top), collapse = "; ")`.

#Cluster 2's average rating of a given concern was `r cluster_means$overall_mean[cluster_means$cluster == 2]`. On average, cluster 2's top concerns were `r paste(shQuote(cluster2_top), collapse = "; ")`.

#Cluster 3's average rating of a given concern was `r cluster_means$overall_mean[cluster_means$cluster == 3]`. On average, cluster 3's top concerns were `r paste(shQuote(cluster3_top), collapse = "; ")`.

```

```{r}
#| label: hierarchical-fig-clusters-avg-willingness   
#| echo: false 
#| fig-cap: Box plot of average willingness to converse about topic by cluster per hierarchical cluster analysis.

avg_willingness_cluster_hierarchical_df <- avg_willingness_df %>%
  merge(concerns_with_cluster_df, by = "prolific_subject_id")

cluster_willingness_boxplot <-
ggplot(avg_willingness_cluster_hierarchical_df, aes(x = factor(clusterID), y = avg_willingness, fill = factor(clusterID))) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(x = "Participant Concern Profile (4 Clusters)", y = "Average Willingness to Converse (0-100)", title = "Willingness to Engage in Conversation by Concern Profile") +
  theme_minimal() +
  geom_text(
    data = hierarchical_cluster_percentages,
    aes(
      y = Inf,
      x = as.factor(clusterID),
      label = paste0("n = ", n)
    ),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    hjust = 0.5,
    vjust = 1,
    size = 3
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("1" = "#951829", "2" = "#951829", "3" = "#951829", "4" = "#951829")) +
    guides(fill = "none")

cluster_willingness_boxplot

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-hierarchical-clusters-avg-openmindedness
#| echo: false 
#| fig-cap: Box plot of average open-mindedness about topic by cluster per hierarchical analysis. 

open_mindedness_w_clusters_hierarchical <- openmindedness_summary_by_subject_df %>%
  merge(concerns_with_cluster_df, by = "prolific_subject_id")

ggplot(open_mindedness_w_clusters_hierarchical, aes(x = factor(clusterID), y = mean_openmindedness, fill = factor(clusterID))) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(x = "Participant Concern Profile (Clusters)", y = "Average Open-Mindedness (1-7)", title = "Average Open-mindedness by Participant Concern Profiles") +
  theme_minimal() + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("1" = "#951829", "2" = "#951829", "3" = "#951829", "4" = "#951829")) +
    guides(fill = "none")


```

```{r}
#| label: fig-demographics-clusters-hierarchical
#| echo: false 
#| fig-cap: Plots of demographics by hierarchical cluster helps us explore whether the clusters may have significantly different demographics.

sex_cluster_plot <- ggplot(surveys_df, aes(x = clusterID, fill = Sex)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  labs(x = "Participant Concern Profile (Cluster)", y = "Proportion of Male and Female Participants", title = "Sex Composition by Participant Concern Profile") + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Male" = "#951829", "Female" = "#63666A"))

  

ethnicity_cluster_plot <- ggplot(surveys_df, aes(x = clusterID, fill = `Ethnicity simplified`)) +
   geom_bar(position = "fill") +
   labs(x = "Participant Concern Profiles (Clusters)", y = "Proportion", title = "Ethnicities Within Participant Concern Profiles") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("White" = "#951829", "Other" = "#63666A", Mixed = "#ED1C24", "Black" = "#D0D0CE", "Asian" = "#F2D4D7"))

age_cluster_plot <- ggplot(
  surveys_df,
  aes(x = factor(clusterID), y = as.numeric(Age), fill = factor(clusterID))
) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    title = "Age Distributions Within Participant Concern Profiles",
    x = "Participant Concern Profiles (Clusters)",
    y = "Age (Years)"
  ) + 
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("1" = "#951829", "2" = "#951829", "3" = "#951829", "4" = "#951829")) +
    guides(fill = "none")

sex_cluster_plot
age_cluster_plot
ethnicity_cluster_plot
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-topic-choice-clusters-hierarchical
#| echo: false 
#| fig-cap: Plots of topic choice by hierarchical cluster helps us explore whether the clusters may have significant differences in which topics they focused on.

# within each cluster, finding percent of subjects on each topic. i tried to do this in one df but kept getting an error that i couldn't figure out 
hierarchical_cluster1_topic_percent <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(clusterID == 1) %>%
  distinct(prolific_subject_id, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  group_by(topicChoiceAsString) %>%
  summarise(n_cluster_1 = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(percent_cluster_1 = n_cluster_1 / sum(n_cluster_1))

hierarchical_cluster2_topic_percent <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(clusterID == 2) %>%
  distinct(prolific_subject_id, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  group_by(topicChoiceAsString) %>%
  summarise(n_cluster_2 = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(percent_cluster_2 = n_cluster_2 / sum(n_cluster_2))

hierarchical_cluster3_topic_percent <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(clusterID == 3) %>%
  distinct(prolific_subject_id, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  group_by(topicChoiceAsString) %>%
  summarise(n_cluster_3 = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(percent_cluster_3 = n_cluster_3 / sum(n_cluster_3))

hierarchical_cluster4_topic_percent <- surveys_df %>%
  filter(clusterID == 4) %>%
  distinct(prolific_subject_id, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  group_by(topicChoiceAsString) %>%
  summarise(n_cluster_4 = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(percent_cluster_4 = n_cluster_4 / sum(n_cluster_4))

#join together
hierarchical_cluster_topic_percent <- hierarchical_cluster1_topic_percent %>%
  full_join(hierarchical_cluster2_topic_percent, by = "topicChoiceAsString") %>% 
  full_join(hierarchical_cluster3_topic_percent, by = "topicChoiceAsString") %>%
 full_join(hierarchical_cluster4_topic_percent, by = "topicChoiceAsString") 

library(forcats)

surveys_df$short_topic_labels <- fct_infreq(surveys_df$short_topic_labels)

ggplot(surveys_df, aes(x = clusterID, fill = short_topic_labels)) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
"U.S. in Israeli-Palestinian conflict" = "#641A2B",                              "Transgender athletes in sports" = "#951829",
"Human euthanasia" = "#ED1C24",
"Capitalism in economy" = "#F2D4D7",
"Climate change policies" = "#FFF8EF",
"Deportation policies" = "#D0D0CE",
"A.I. in human-run spaces" = "#808080",
"Vaccine Mandates" = "#63666A",
"Police conduct and accountability" = "#C6AA76",
"Government in healthcare" = "#000080"
)) +
  labs(x = "Participant Concern Profiles (Clusters)", y = "Proportion", title = "Focus Topic Distributions Within Participant Concern Profiles", fill = "Focus topic")

```

```{r}
#| label: Look for sign diffs in sex btwn clusters
#| echo: false

# make a df that only has one row per subject
sig_dif_df <- surveys_df %>%
  distinct(prolific_subject_id, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(Age = as.numeric(Age))

sex_table <- table(sig_dif_df$clusterID, sig_dif_df$Sex)
chisq_sex <- chisq.test(sex_table)
# p value isn't significant 

```

```{r}
#| label: Look for sign diffs in ethnicity btwn clusters
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false 

eth_table <- table(sig_dif_df$clusterID, sig_dif_df$`Ethnicity simplified`)

#there are too few people in the third and fourth cluster, so i am going to group minority ethnicities together. the oversimplification is not ideal but at least it's something 
eth_chisq_df <- sig_dif_df %>%
  mutate(
    ethnicity_collapsed = case_when(
      `Ethnicity simplified` %in% c("Black", "Mixed", "Other", "Asian") ~ "Minority",
      `Ethnicity simplified` == "White" ~ "White"
    )
  )

eth_simplified_table <- table(eth_chisq_df$clusterID, eth_chisq_df$ethnicity_collapsed)
chisq_ethnicity <- chisq.test(eth_simplified_table)
# not significant


# gonna look at collapsing cluster similar (by concern profile) clusters instead of race  
eth_chisq_2_df <- sig_dif_df %>%
  mutate(collapsed_clusterID = case_when(
    clusterID == 1 ~ "one_and_two",
    clusterID == 2 ~ "one_and_two",
    clusterID == 3 ~ "three_and_four",
    clusterID == 4 ~ "three_and_four",
    .default = NA
  ))

eth_simplified_table_2 <- table(eth_chisq_2_df$collapsed_clusterID, eth_chisq_2_df$`Ethnicity simplified`)
chisq_ethnicity_2 <- chisq.test(eth_simplified_table_2)
# still too few obs. could do more extensive methods, but i don't think it's worth it here

```

```{r}
#| label: Look for sign diffs in focus topic btwn clusters
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false 

# topic choice
topic_table <- table(sig_dif_df$clusterID, sig_dif_df$short_topic_labels)
#there are too few obs in a lot of entries, so i'm going to collapse the less common topics together 

# identify rare topics dynamically
rare_topics <- topic_frequency_counts %>%
  filter(n < 30) %>% # min count topics 
  pull(short_topic_labels)

# collapse clusters 3 and 4 and collapse low count topics because there are too few counts to run chi sq test
topic_chisq_df <- sig_dif_df %>%
  mutate(collapsed_clusterID = case_when(
    clusterID == 1 ~ "one",
    clusterID == 2 ~ "two",
    clusterID == 3 ~ "three_and_four",
    clusterID == 4 ~ "three_and_four",
    .default = NA
  )) %>%
  mutate(topic_collapsed = ifelse(short_topic_labels %in% rare_topics, 
                             "Other", 
                             short_topic_labels)) 
topic_table_coll <- table(topic_chisq_df$collapsed_clusterID, topic_chisq_df$topic_collapsed)

chisq_topic_coll <- chisq.test(topic_table_coll)
#insignificant p value. the data has become so simplified anyway
```

```{r}
#| label: Look for sig diffs in age btwn clusters
#| echo: false

# do anova and nonparametric just in case 
anova_age <- aov(Age ~ factor(clusterID), data = sig_dif_df)

summary_anova_age <- summary(anova_age)

nonpara_age <- kruskal.test(Age ~ factor(clusterID), data = sig_dif_df)

#neither significant 

library(car)
leveneTest(Age ~ factor(clusterID), data = sig_dif_df)
```

A chi-square test of independence indicated that sex did not significantly differ across clusters, χ²(`r chisq_sex$parameter`, N = `r sum(sex_table)`) = `r round(chisq_sex$statistic, 2)`, p = `r round(chisq_sex$p.value, 3)`.

A chi-square test of independence indicated that ethnicity did not significantly differ across clusters, χ²(`r chisq_ethnicity$parameter`, N = `r sum(eth_simplified_table)`) = `r round(chisq_ethnicity$statistic, 2)`, p = `r round(chisq_ethnicity$p.value, 3)`.

A one-way ANOVA indicated that age did not significantly differ across clusters, F(`r summary_anova_age[[1]]$Df[1]`, `r summary_anova_age[[1]]$Df[2]`) = `r round(summary_anova_age[[1]]$"F value"[1], 2)`, p `r ifelse(summary_anova_age[[1]]$"Pr(>F)"[1] < .001, "< .001", paste0("= ", sub("^0", "", format(round(summary_anova_age[[1]]$"Pr(>F)"[1], 3), nsmall = 3))))`.

### Correlations

```{r}
#| label: Function for printing correlation results
#| echo: false

print_significant_correlations <- function(df) {
  formatted_items <- df %>%
    filter(significance != "ns") %>%
    mutate(
      formatted = paste0(
       '"', question, '"',
        " ($r$ = ", estimate_rounded,
        ", $p$ = ", significance, ")"
      )
    ) %>%
    pull(formatted)
  
  n <- length(formatted_items)
  
  if (n == 0) {
    return("No significant correlations found.")
  } else if (n == 1) {
    return(formatted_items)
  } else if (n == 2) {
    return(paste(formatted_items, collapse = " and "))
  } else {
    return(paste(
      paste(formatted_items[-n], collapse = ", "),
      "and", formatted_items[n]
    ))
  }
}

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-EDA-correlation-openminded-relationships 
#| echo: false 
#| fig-cap: Relationships between each willingness concern and average open-mindedness. Using a 7-pt Likert-scale, participants rated the impact of each willingness concern on their overall willingness. Also using a 7-pt Likert-scale, participants rated 5 statements about their open-mindedness, which we averaged to find each participant's average open-mindedness.  

library(gridExtra) # so we can print the plots in columns 

cor_openmindedness_mean_vs_concerns <- concerns_wide_na_replaced %>% map_dfr(~ tidy(cor.test(.x, openmindedness_summary_by_subject_df$mean_openmindedness)), .id = "concern")

concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df <- openmindedness_summary_by_subject_df %>%
  select(mean_openmindedness, prolific_subject_id) %>%
  full_join(concerns_labeled_wide_na_replaced, by = "prolific_subject_id") %>%
  select(-"prolific_subject_id")

# see the average number of concerns that people indicated as affecting them 

# Create all the individual plots
p1.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q01, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p2.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q02, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p3.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q03, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p4.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q04, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p5.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q05, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p6.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q06, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p7.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q07, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p8.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q08, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p9.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q09, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p10.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q010, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p11.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q011, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p12.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q012, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p13.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q013, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p14.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q014, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p15.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q015, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p16.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q016, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p17.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q017, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p18.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q018, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p19.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q019, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p20.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q020, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p21.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q021, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p22.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q022, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p23.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q023, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p24.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q024, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p25.o <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q025, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)


# arrange the plots in a grid
#suppressWarnings(grid.arrange(p1.o, p2.o, p3.o, p4.o, p5.o, p6.o, p7.o, p8.o, p9.o, p10.o, p11.o, p12.o, p13.o, p14.o, p15.o, p16.o, p17.o, p18.o, p19.o, p20.o, p21.o, p22.o, p23.o, p24.o, p25.o, ncol = 4))


### the results seem to pretty much show no relationship, but if you squint your eyes, there is vaguely a linear relationship in some of the variables. i'm going to stick with pearson because, to my knowledge, the data doesn't meet the standards for any non-parametric methods 

#print example plot since printing all of them is way too much
ggplot(concerns_and_avg_openmindedness_df, aes(x = Q011, y = mean_openmindedness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0) + 
  labs(x = "Rating of concern item's affect on willingness", y = "Open-mindedness rating", title = "Open-mindedness vs. severity of concern's effect on willingness")

```

```{r}
#| label: Correlations open-mindedness average vs. willingness concerns (NA is not replaced)
#| include: false
#| tbl-cap: Correlation matrix of average open-mindedness vs. willingness concerns 

# look at correlation relationship between mean open-mindedness score and each willingness concern
library(broom)

cor_openmindedness_mean_vs_concerns <- concerns_wide_na_replaced %>% map_dfr(~ tidy(cor.test(.x, openmindedness_summary_by_subject_df$mean_openmindedness)), .id = "concern")

# view results
cor_openmindedness_mean_vs_concerns_simplified <- cor_openmindedness_mean_vs_concerns %>%
  select(concern, estimate, p.value) %>%
  arrange(p.value) %>% 
  full_join(question_key_for_fa, by = c("concern" = "short_label"))
```

```{r}
#| label: Correlations open-mindedness average vs. willingness concerns NA replaced
#| echo: false


# using the df that replaced empty entries with "not at all" because this is conceptually correct
cor_openmindedness_mean_vs_concerns_na_replaced <- concerns_wide_na_replaced %>% map_dfr(~ tidy(cor.test(.x, openmindedness_summary_by_subject_df$mean_openmindedness)), .id = "concern") 

# view results
cor_openmindedness_mean_vs_concerns_simplified_na_replaced <- cor_openmindedness_mean_vs_concerns_na_replaced %>% 
    mutate(
    significance = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "< 0.001",
      p.value < 0.01  ~ "< 0.01",
      p.value < 0.05  ~ "< 0.05",
      TRUE            ~ "ns"  # not significant
    ),
    direction = if_else(estimate < 0, "negative", "positive"),
    estimate_rounded = round(estimate, 3),
    pvalue_rounded = signif(p.value, 3)
  ) %>%
  select(concern, estimate_rounded, pvalue_rounded, direction, significance) %>%
  full_join(question_key_for_fa, by = c("concern" = "short_label")) %>%
    mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('unwillingness-reason-', '', .))) 

```

Accounting for the concerns that participants did not endorse as "not at all" impacting participants' willingness, we found some significant correlations between willingness concerns and average open-mindedness. 

These significant concerns were `r print_significant_correlations(cor_openmindedness_mean_vs_concerns_simplified_na_replaced)`.

```{r}
#| label: fig-EDA-correlation-relationships 
#| echo: false 
#| fig-cap: Relationships between each willingness concern and average willingness to converse. Using a 7-pt Likert-scale, participants rated the impact of each willingness concern on their overall willingness. Using a slider from 0 to 100, participants rated 2 statements about their willingness to converse, which we averaged to find each participant's average willingness to converse about their focus topic. 

library(gridExtra) # so we can print the plots in columns 

concerns_and_avg_willingness_df <- concerns_labeled_wide %>%
  full_join(avg_willingness_df, by = "prolific_subject_id")

# see the average number of concerns that people indicated as affecting them 

# Create all the individual plots
p1 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q01, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p2 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q02, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p3 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q03, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p4 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q04, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p5 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q05, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p6 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q06, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p7 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q07, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p8 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q08, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p9 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q09, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p10 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q010, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p11 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q011, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p12 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q012, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p13 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q013, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p14 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q014, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p15 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q015, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p16 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q016, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p17 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q017, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p18 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q018, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p19 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q019, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p20 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q020, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p21 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q021, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p22 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q022, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p23 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q023, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p24 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q024, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)
p25 <- ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q025, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0)

# arrange the plots in a 2x8 grid
#suppressWarnings(grid.arrange(p1, p2, p3, p4, p5, p6, p7, p8, p9, p10, p11, p12, p13, p14, p15, p16, p17, p18, p19, p20, p21, p22, p23, p24, p25, ncol = 2))


### the results seem to pretty much show no relationship, but if you squint your eyes, there is vaguely a linear relationship in some of the variables. i'm going to stick with pearson because, to my knowledge, the data doesn't meet the standards for any non-parametric methods 


# print an example plot
ggplot(concerns_and_avg_willingness_df, aes(x = Q04, y = avg_willingness)) + geom_point() + geom_jitter(width = 0.2, height = 0) + labs(x = "Rating of concern item's effect on willingness", y = "Open-mindedness", title = "Open-mindedness vs. severity of concern item effect on willingness")
```

```{r}
#| label: Correlations willingness slider average vs. willingness concerns (NA is not replaced) 
#| echo: false

#prepping data
reasons_and_willingness_merged_df <- merge(concerns_labeled_wide, avg_willingness_df, by = 'prolific_subject_id') %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(-prolific_subject_id, -topicChoiceAsString)

#run cor test
cor_willingness_slider_vs_concerns <- reasons_and_willingness_merged_df %>% select(-avg_willingness) %>%
  map_dfr( ~ tidy(cor.test(.x, reasons_and_willingness_merged_df$avg_willingness)),
  .id = "reason_item"
)

#process results 
cor_willingness_slider_vs_concerns <- cor_willingness_slider_vs_concerns %>%
  mutate(
    significance = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "< 0.001",
      p.value < 0.01  ~ "< 0.01",
      p.value < 0.05  ~ "< 0.05",
      TRUE            ~ "ns"  # not significant
    ),
    direction = if_else(estimate < 0, "negative", "positive"),
    estimate_rounded = round(estimate, 3),
    pvalue_rounded = signif(p.value, 3)
  ) %>%
  select(reason_item, estimate_rounded, pvalue_rounded, direction, significance)

```

```{r}
#| label: Correlations willingness slider average vs. willingness concerns NA replaced 
#| echo: false

#prepping data
reasons_and_willingness_merged_df_na_replaced <- merge(concerns_labeled_wide_na_replaced, avg_willingness_df, by = 'prolific_subject_id') %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(-prolific_subject_id, -topicChoiceAsString)

#run cor test
cor_willingness_slider_vs_concerns_na_replaced_d <- reasons_and_willingness_merged_df_na_replaced %>% select(-avg_willingness) %>%
  map_dfr( ~ tidy(cor.test(.x, reasons_and_willingness_merged_df_na_replaced$avg_willingness)),
  .id = "reason_item"
)

#process results  ### LEFT OFF PRINTING THE SIGNIFICANT ONES 
cor_willingness_slider_vs_concerns_na_replaced <- cor_willingness_slider_vs_concerns_na_replaced_d %>%
  mutate(
    significance = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "< 0.001",
      p.value < 0.01  ~ "< 0.01",
      p.value < 0.05  ~ "< 0.05",
      TRUE            ~ "ns"  # not significant
    ),
    direction = if_else(estimate < 0, "negative", "positive"),
    estimate_rounded = round(estimate, 3),
    pvalue_rounded = signif(p.value, 3)
  ) %>%
  select(reason_item, estimate_rounded, pvalue_rounded, direction, significance) %>%
  full_join(question_key_for_fa, by = c("reason_item" = "short_label")) %>%
    mutate(across(everything(), ~ gsub('unwillingness-reason-', '', .))) 

```

Next, we looked for correlations between each willingness concern and average willingness as indicated by the two slider questions. Many concerns were negatively associated with willingness, suggesting that, for most of the willingness concerns, a greater impact of the concern on an American's willingness is associated with lower willingness to engage.

These concerns were `r print_significant_correlations(cor_willingness_slider_vs_concerns_na_replaced)`.

### Free response

To assess the comprehensiveness of the inventory, we analyzed the free-response data using LLMs with manual review on 10% of the sample for validation. \[The LLMs detected some potentially unique concerns, but ultimately we think the questionnaire does sufficently capture concerns. Should expand on what I mean here- how specific about what the LLMs detected?\]

## Discussion

This pretest study aimed to assess the comprehensiveness and underlying structure of a newly developed 25-item questionnaire designed to capture the core concerns contributing to Americans' unwillingness to engage in open, productive discussions on divisive topics. Our findings validate the instrument's relevance and offer a preliminary structural framework, while also highlighting key areas for refinement.

### Comprehensiveness of Concerns

Our first objective was to determine if the 25-item inventory comprehensively captured the reasons for reluctance to engage. Through a qualitative analysis of participants' free-response explanations for avoiding difficult conversations, we found that [results].

### Structure of Concerns

Our second objective was to explore the latent structure of the 25 concerns. An Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA) suggested a four-factor solution, which accounted for `r total_var_explained_percent`% (not sure) of the total variance. These factors were conceptually labeled as:

\[insert\]

### Relationships to Willingness and Open-Mindedness

[results]

### Cluster Analysis

Our $k$-means cluster analysis suggested that participants' willingness concerns generally fell into one of five distinct clusters that were differentiated by \[?\]. Our hierarchical cluster analysis suggested that participants' willingness concerns generally fell into one of three distinct cluster that were differentiated by \[?\]

### Limitations and Future Directions

The primary limitation of this study is the reliance on self-report measures for all key variables (concerns, willingness, and open-mindedness). The discrepancy between the Likert-scale comfort ratings and the slider-based willingness ratings (both asked about the focus topic), for example, highlights the inherent difficulties in measuring these constructs. Future work should integrate behavioral or implicit measures to provide a more objective assessment.

In conclusion, this pretest confirms that the 25-item questionnaire, particularly with the proposed additions, is a valuable tool for measuring the multifaceted barriers to productive discourse on divisive topics. The identified factor structure and participant clusters will be crucial for tailoring our individual-level intervention to specifically address the emotional, efficacy, and social concerns that drive an American's unwillingness to engage.

## References
